previous_plan.title: Precision Nitrogen Prescription Prototype v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A research prototype built on Vue 3, Flask, and PostGIS to process DJI drone multispectral imagery into NDVI-driven variable-rate nitrogen prescription maps for corn field trials. Llama 3.1 on Ollama generates agronomic summaries locally.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Vue 3 SPA communicating with Flask REST API over lab HTTPS. Celery workers handle async NDVI raster computation on GPU. PostGIS stores field boundaries and prescription zone geometries.
previous_plan.data_model: flight_missions, ndvi_maps, prescription_zones, field_boundaries. No calibration entities defined. No sensor_calibrations table.
previous_plan.api_design: POST /missions (upload TIFF), GET /missions/:id/ndvi, POST /prescriptions (generate zones), GET /prescriptions/:id/export. No POST /auth/refresh endpoint.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision targets two blocking issues: sensor calibration panel integration and Llama 3.1 70B VRAM feasibility. Adding sensor_calibrations entity with empirical line correction coefficients and inserting the calibration step before NDVI band math makes multi-flight data scientifically comparable. Specifying Q4_K_M GGUF quantization (~10GB active VRAM) resolves hardware feasibility. Adding POST /auth/refresh with httpOnly refresh cookie closes the session expiry gap. The PostGIS and Celery architecture from round 1 is retained.

fix_report.action_taken: Added sensor_calibrations entity (mission_id FK, band_name, panel_reflectance, gain, offset, applied_at). Backend raster pipeline now executes empirical line calibration step before NDVI computation, converting raw DN values to surface reflectance using panel targets. calibration_panel_path added to flight_missions. calibration_applied BOOL flag enables fallback warning when panel not provided.
fix_report.expected_outcome: NDVI values across flights will be radiometrically comparable, enabling valid multi-flight nitrogen stress trend analysis

fix_report.action_taken: Specified Llama 3.1 70B Q4_K_M GGUF quantization (~42GB on disk, ~10GB active VRAM) fitting A4000 16GB VRAM. Added to technology_stack and llm_integration sections. Inference latency (~45 seconds per prescription) documented as acceptable for research use. Quality tradeoff versus fp16 noted in risks_and_tradeoffs.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Ollama deployment is feasible on lab hardware; quantization tradeoff documented for grant reporting

target_output.title: Precision Nitrogen Prescription Prototype v2
target_output.executive_summary: A research prototype on Vue 3, Flask, PostGIS, and Celery that processes DJI drone multispectral TIFFs through per-flight radiometric calibration into NDVI/NDRE-driven variable-rate nitrogen prescription maps for corn field trials. Llama 3.1 70B Q4_K_M on Ollama generates agronomic zone summaries entirely on-premise. Runs on a 64GB RAM NVIDIA A4000 bare-metal lab server with zero cloud egress of raw imagery.
target_output.architecture_overview: Vue 3 TypeScript SPA served via Nginx communicates over lab HTTPS to Flask REST API. Multipart TIFF uploads land on NFS at /data/missions/. Celery workers in a GPU-enabled Docker container pull calibration and NDVI computation jobs from Redis, executing empirical line correction then GPU-accelerated NDVI band math via CuPy. Processed prescription zone polygons are written to PostGIS with GEOMETRY(Polygon,4326) columns. Leaflet.js renders GeoJSON zone overlays on a satellite basemap. A dedicated Ollama container serves Llama 3.1 70B Q4_K_M for agronomic summary inference on completed prescription records. Grafana and Prometheus run as sidecar containers monitoring job throughput and GPU utilization.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Vue 3 with TypeScript, Pinia, Vue Router 4, Leaflet.js 1.9 with GeoJSON overlay plugin. Backend: Python 3.12, Flask 3.0, Flask-JWT-Extended, Celery 5.3 with Redis 7 broker, GDAL 3.8, Rasterio 1.3, Shapely 2.0, CuPy 13 for NVIDIA GPU band math, Fiona for shapefile export, lxml for ISOXML generation. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with PostGIS 3.4; NFS for raw TIFF and GeoTIFF storage. LLM: Ollama 0.3 serving Llama 3.1 70B Q4_K_M GGUF (42GB model, ~10GB active VRAM on A4000 16GB). Monitoring: Grafana 10, Prometheus, nvidia-smi Prometheus exporter, Celery Flower.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Flight Mission Management: upload DJI Zenmuse P1 multispectral TIFFs with flight metadata (date, drone model, pilot) and calibration panel images; track per-mission processing status from queued through complete. Radiometric Calibration Engine: apply per-band empirical line calibration using panel reflectance targets to convert raw DN to surface reflectance before any index computation. NDVI/NDRE Processor: GPU-accelerated band math producing calibrated index GeoTIFFs stored on NFS. Prescription Zone Editor: interactive Leaflet map for reviewing k-means segmented zones and adjusting per-zone nitrogen_rate_kg_ha values. Agronomic Summary Generator: Ollama Llama 3.1 70B produces plain-language nitrogen status interpretation per zone from NDVI statistics and phenology stage. Export Engine: GeoTIFF, shapefile (Fiona), and ISOXML variable-rate prescription file generation for planter controller compatibility.
target_output.system_components: Nginx reverse proxy (HTTPS termination, static Vue asset serving), Flask API container (REST endpoints, JWT auth, multipart upload routing), Celery Worker container (NVIDIA runtime, GPU-accelerated band math, calibration pipeline, LLM job queuing), Redis 7 container (Celery broker and result backend), PostgreSQL 16 + PostGIS 3.4 container (spatial data store), Ollama container (Llama 3.1 70B Q4_K_M inference server), NFS mount /data/missions/ (raw TIFFs, processed GeoTIFFs, export zips), Grafana + Prometheus containers, nvidia-smi Prometheus exporter.
target_output.workflows: Calibrated NDVI Processing: Agronomist uploads mission via Vue wizard selecting multispectral TIFFs and calibration panel images. Flask API writes flight_missions record, stores TIFFs to NFS, queues Celery task chain: (1) load panel images, compute per-band mean panel reflectance, fit empirical line gain/offset coefficients, persist to sensor_calibrations; (2) apply correction to raw band arrays using CuPy, compute NDVI=(NIR-Red)/(NIR+Red) and NDRE=(RedEdge-Red)/(RedEdge+Red), write GeoTIFF to NFS, insert ndvi_maps record; (3) run k-means on NDVI raster to delineate prescription_zones polygons, insert PostGIS geometries with zone_class and ndvi_mean; (4) call Ollama with zone statistics and phenology stage, store agronomic_summary on each zone. Prescription Export: Agronomist opens Leaflet prescription editor, adjusts per-zone nitrogen_rate_kg_ha, selects export formats and clicks Export. Flask generates GeoTIFF via GDAL, shapefile via Fiona, and ISOXML using lxml template, bundles zip at /data/exports/:id/, returns signed download URL.
target_output.data_model: flight_missions (id UUID PK, field_id FK, pilot_name, drone_model, flight_date, raw_tiff_path TEXT, calibration_panel_path TEXT, processing_status ENUM queued/processing/complete/failed, calibration_applied BOOL DEFAULT false, processing_errors JSONB, created_at). sensor_calibrations (id UUID PK, mission_id FK, band_name ENUM red/green/blue/red_edge/nir, panel_reflectance FLOAT, gain FLOAT, offset FLOAT, irradiance_log_path TEXT, applied_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ndvi_maps (id UUID PK, mission_id FK, output_geotiff_path TEXT, ndvi_min FLOAT, ndvi_max FLOAT, ndvi_mean FLOAT, computed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). prescription_zones (id UUID PK, ndvi_map_id FK, geom GEOMETRY(Polygon,4326), ndvi_mean FLOAT, nitrogen_rate_kg_ha FLOAT, zone_class ENUM deficient/adequate/excess, agronomic_summary TEXT). field_boundaries (id UUID PK, field_name TEXT, grower_name TEXT, geom GEOMETRY(MultiPolygon,4326), area_ha FLOAT). prescription_exports (id UUID PK, prescription_id FK, export_format ENUM geotiff/shapefile/isoxml, file_path TEXT, exported_by UUID, exported_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: POST /auth/login (credentials JSON, returns access+refresh tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Strict cookies). POST /auth/refresh (refresh token cookie, returns new 8-hour access token). POST /missions (multipart: metadata JSON + multispectral TIFFs + panel images, returns {mission_id, job_id}). GET /jobs/:id/status (Celery task state, progress 0-100, current_step). GET /ndvi/:mission_id/geojson (prescription zones as GeoJSON FeatureCollection for Leaflet). PATCH /prescriptions/:id/zones/:zone_id (body: {nitrogen_rate_kg_ha}, updates rate). POST /prescriptions/:id/export (body: {formats: [geotiff,shapefile,isoxml]}, returns {download_url}). GET /fields/:id/boundary (field boundary GeoJSON).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Flask-JWT-Extended with 8-hour access tokens and 7-day refresh tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Strict cookies. CSRF double-submit cookie protection on all state-mutating endpoints. RBAC enforced via JWT role claim: researcher (create missions, export, delete), agronomist (view and annotate only). HTTPS enforced by Nginx with lab CA certificate; HTTP requests redirected to HTTPS. Ollama runs air-gapped on lab Docker bridge network with no external route. Raw TIFF data never transmitted off bare-metal server enforced by Docker network policy and university firewall rules. PostGIS credentials and Ollama URL in .env excluded from GitLab repository. FAA Part 107 compliance is an operational pilot certification requirement, not a software control.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Docker Compose on bare-metal with named volumes: postgres_data, redis_data; NFS bind mounts for /data/missions/ and /data/exports/. GitLab CI pipeline: (1) pytest unit tests for calibration coefficient computation, NDVI band math with synthetic 4-band TIFF fixture, and zone segmentation logic; (2) flake8 linting; (3) Docker build and push to lab GitLab Container Registry; (4) SSH deploy job: docker compose pull followed by docker compose up -d on bare-metal server via deploy key. Environments: dev (port 5001), staging (port 5002), production (port 443 via Nginx) on same server isolated by Docker bridge networks. Database migrations via Alembic run as docker compose run flask-api flask db upgrade pre-deploy step. No automated rollback; manual docker compose rollback to previous image tag.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes Flask /metrics (HTTP request count and latency histograms by endpoint and status code), Celery Flower (active task count, task duration histogram by task name), and nvidia-smi exporter (GPU utilization %, VRAM used MB per container). Grafana dashboards: NDVI Processing Throughput (missions per day, p50/p95 job duration), GPU VRAM Utilization during Celery raster jobs, Prescription Export volume by format. Alert rules: Celery worker GPU OOM error rate > 2 per hour, NDVI job queue depth > 10 pending tasks for > 30 minutes, PostgreSQL connection count > 80% of max_connections.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost; all compute on grant-funded bare-metal. A4000 GPU handles approximately 4 concurrent NDVI band math jobs before VRAM saturation at 16GB. Ollama inference for agronomic summaries runs sequentially at ~45 seconds per prescription at Q4_K_M; parallel inference not viable on single GPU. NFS capacity planning: 300MB per mission TIFF set; 200 missions per growing season equals approximately 60GB per year on lab NAS. PostGIS query performance adequate for research-scale data (< 10,000 prescription zone polygons).
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Calibration and NDVI Core (6 weeks, spring planting): Flight mission upload with panel image selection, radiometric calibration pipeline with empirical line correction, NDVI/NDRE GeoTIFF generation, PostGIS prescription zone storage, basic Vue Leaflet viewer with zone color ramp. Phase 2 - Prescription Editor and LLM Integration (4 weeks, early canopy): Per-zone nitrogen rate editing in Leaflet, Ollama agronomic summary generation with Q4_K_M Llama 3.1 70B, prescription export in GeoTIFF and shapefile formats. Phase 3 - ISOXML Export and Multi-Season Analytics (4 weeks, pre-harvest): ISOXML variable-rate controller file generation, multi-flight NDVI trend comparison charts, yield correlation data ingestion for model validation, grant reporting analytics dashboard.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python type hints required for all Flask route functions and Celery task signatures. pytest coverage must reach 85% for calibration coefficient computation and NDVI band math modules. GDAL dataset objects wrapped in context managers to prevent file handle leaks. CuPy arrays transferred back to NumPy before PostGIS insertion via psycopg2. LLM prompt templates versioned in /prompts/ directory with semantic versioning. GitLab MR requires one senior researcher approval. Synthetic 4-band test TIFFs generated by test utility; no real mission data committed to repo.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Q4_K_M quantization reduces Llama 3.1 70B agronomic summary quality versus fp16. Mitigation: evaluate summaries against expert agronomist annotations in Phase 2; downgrade to Llama 3 8B if quality insufficient for grant deliverables. Risk: Empirical line calibration requires one calibration panel image per flight; missions without panel images fall back to uncalibrated NDVI with calibration_applied=False warning flag. Risk: Docker Compose single-host has no HA; daily pg_dump to lab NAS is the sole recovery path. Tradeoff: k-means zone segmentation over supervised classification chosen for reproducibility without training labels; researchers can adjust zone count parameter per field.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected empirical line calibration over flat-field correction because DJI Zenmuse P1 missions consistently include calibration panel images and irradiance sensor logs. Confirmed Q4_K_M GGUF for Llama 3.1 70B over Llama 3 8B for richer agronomic reasoning depth; quality validation deferred to Phase 2 expert annotation comparison. ISOXML format chosen over John Deere proprietary RX format for cross-brand variable-rate controller compatibility. POST /auth/refresh added with httpOnly refresh cookie pattern consistent with lab HTTPS-only deployment.

metadata.notes: Precision agriculture research prototype; round 2 resolves calibration pipeline gap and LLM VRAM feasibility on A4000.

project.goal: Least-cost ration formulation via LP optimization for feed mill nutritionists
project.target_users: Feed mill nutritionists and production managers
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks, full release in 20 weeks

frontend.state: Zustand for ingredient and ration editor state

backend.ipc: Electron main process spawns Python subprocess with JSON stdin/stdout IPC

security.auth: Local password store with bcrypt hashing

data.orm: SQLAlchemy for Python data access layer

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for test and build
devops.packaging: Electron Builder with NSIS Windows installer
devops.environments: dev (npm run electron:dev), production (packaged installer)

open_questions.pdf_library: ReportLab vs WeasyPrint for PDF spec sheet generation

reasoner_reviews.product: The least-cost ration formulation workflow is the core value proposition; the LP solver must expose constraint violation feedback when no feasible solution exists (e.g., minimum CP requirement cannot be met with available ingredients at specified minimums). Nutritionists need to understand which constraints are binding so they can relax ingredient inclusion limits intelligently.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Electron with a Python subprocess for LP solving is the right separation of concerns. The React UI handles ingredient selection and constraint entry while Python SciPy handles the LP via JSON IPC. SQLCipher on SQLite protects proprietary ingredient cost data adequately for an offline workstation. Using Zustand rather than Redux for state keeps the bundle lean for a desktop app.
reasoner_reviews.security: bcrypt for local password hashing and SQLCipher for database encryption are appropriate controls for a local_only application. The main security risk is unauthorized access to the workstation itself, which is an IT/physical control. No network attack surface exists. Ensure the SQLCipher key is derived from the user password rather than hardcoded.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: SciPy linprog with the HiGHS backend handles LP problems with 100+ ingredients and 50+ nutritional constraints in under 100ms, well within interactive response time requirements. PDF export via ReportLab is preferred over WeasyPrint due to no runtime HTML dependency and smaller packaged size.

backend.service_design: Python solver module with three components: lp_formulator (builds scipy.optimize.linprog problem matrices from ingredient and constraint inputs), result_parser (converts LP solution vector to ingredient inclusion percentages), pdf_exporter (generates PDF spec sheets via ReportLab)
backend.api_patterns: JSON stdin/stdout IPC between Electron main process and Python subprocess; Electron sends {action: 'solve', payload: {ingredients, constraints}} and receives {status: 'optimal'|'infeasible', solution: {...}, shadow_prices: {...}}
backend.business_modules: Ingredient library CRUD (nutritional profile management), species requirement profiles (broiler starter/grower/finisher, layer, swine, cattle), ration version management with diff comparison, cost history tracking per ingredient
backend.failure_handling: LP infeasibility returns binding constraint list and shadow prices to UI so nutritionist sees which nutrient minimum cannot be satisfied; Python exceptions caught and returned as {status: 'error', message: ...} JSON to Electron

frontend.app_structure: Electron main process manages Python subprocess lifecycle and SQLCipher DB connection; React renderer process displays ingredient selection tables, constraint editors, and solution visualization; preload.js exposes contextBridge API for IPC
frontend.pages_and_flows: Ingredient Library (CRUD grid with nutritional profile editor), Species Profiles (requirement set editor per production stage), Ration Formulator (ingredient selection checklist, min/max inclusion bounds, LP solve button, solution table with inclusion %, cost breakdown), Ration History (versioned list with side-by-side diff), Export (PDF spec sheet preview and save)
frontend.state_management: Zustand stores: ingredientLibrary (list with nutritional profiles), rationSession (selected ingredients, constraints, last solution), exportQueue (pending PDF jobs)

security.auth_design: Local login screen with username/password; bcrypt hash comparison against users table in SQLCipher DB; session token stored in Electron app memory only, never persisted to disk
security.authorization_model: Nutritionist role: full access to formulation and export. Manager role: view-only access to ration history and cost reports. Role checked in preload contextBridge before exposing IPC methods.
security.secrets_management: SQLCipher encryption key derived from user password via PBKDF2 with per-DB salt stored in a separate plaintext file; this means the DB is locked when no user is logged in

data.entities: feed_ingredients (id INTEGER PK, name TEXT, supplier TEXT, dry_matter_pct REAL, crude_protein_pct REAL, metabolizable_energy_kcal_kg REAL, fat_pct REAL, fiber_pct REAL, lysine_pct REAL, methionine_pct REAL, phosphorus_pct REAL, calcium_pct REAL, created_at TEXT). nutritional_profiles (id, ingredient_id FK, nutrient_name TEXT, value REAL, unit TEXT). animal_species_requirements (id, species TEXT, production_stage TEXT, nutrient_name TEXT, min_value REAL, max_value REAL). ration_formulas (id, name TEXT, species TEXT, production_stage TEXT, created_by TEXT, created_at TEXT, version INTEGER). ration_ingredients (id, formula_id FK, ingredient_id FK, inclusion_pct REAL, min_inclusion REAL, max_inclusion REAL). ingredient_inventory (id, ingredient_id FK, quantity_kg REAL, cost_per_kg REAL, recorded_date TEXT). cost_records (id, ingredient_id FK, cost_per_kg REAL, effective_date TEXT).
data.storage_design: Single SQLite file with SQLCipher encryption at %APPDATA%/FeedMill/rations.db; WAL mode for concurrent read during LP solve; Python accesses via SQLAlchemy, Electron reads via better-sqlite3 in main process for React state hydration

devops.deployment_topology: Electron Builder produces NSIS Windows installer bundling: Electron runtime, React build, Python 3.12 embedded interpreter with SciPy, ReportLab, SQLAlchemy; single-file installer ~120MB
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest for LP formulator unit tests (verify optimal solutions for 10 reference ration problems), Jest for React component tests, Electron Builder Windows build on windows-latest runner, artifact upload of installer .exe
devops.environments: Development: npm run electron:dev with Vite HMR and Python subprocess in dev mode. Production: packaged NSIS installer with embedded Python runtime.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing an offline Windows desktop application using Electron + React for the UI and a Python subprocess for LP-based ration optimization. SQLCipher on SQLite secures proprietary ingredient cost data. Key architectural decision is the JSON IPC boundary between Electron main process and Python solver, which keeps the LP engine replaceable without touching the UI. The feed_ingredients and ration_formulas entities must capture all nutritional attributes needed by the HiGHS LP backend.
target_output.title: Feed Mill Least-Cost Ration Formulator Desktop Application
target_output.executive_summary: An offline Windows desktop application for feed mill nutritionists that computes least-cost animal feed rations using SciPy linear programming (HiGHS backend) against a local ingredient library, inventory costs, and species-specific nutritional requirement profiles. Built with Electron 30 and React 18 for the UI and a Python 3.12 subprocess for LP solving and PDF export. All data persists in a SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite database on the workstation. No internet connectivity required.
target_output.architecture_overview: Electron main process manages three responsibilities: SQLCipher database connection via better-sqlite3, Python subprocess lifecycle (spawned on startup, communicates via JSON stdin/stdout), and file system access for PDF export. The React renderer process communicates with main via contextBridge IPC API, keeping all privileged operations in the main process. Python solver module receives ingredient inclusion bounds and species nutritional constraints as JSON, invokes scipy.optimize.linprog with the HiGHS simplex backend, and returns the optimal solution vector with shadow prices for binding constraints. Zustand manages React state for the ingredient session and ration formulator. Electron Builder packages Python 3.12 as an embedded interpreter alongside the Node runtime in the NSIS installer.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 30, React 18 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand, Vite for renderer bundling. Backend: Python 3.12, SciPy 1.13 (linprog HiGHS), SQLAlchemy 2.0 for Python ORM, ReportLab 4.0 for PDF generation. Database: SQLite with SQLCipher 4.6 encryption, better-sqlite3 for Electron main process access. Packaging: Electron Builder with embedded Python 3.12 runtime, NSIS Windows installer. Testing: pytest for LP formulator, Jest + React Testing Library for components. CI: GitHub Actions on windows-latest runner.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Ingredient Library: add/edit/delete feed ingredients with full nutritional profiles (CP, ME, fat, fiber, lysine, methionine, P, Ca); bulk import from CSV. Inventory and Cost Management: enter current ingredient availability (kg) and cost per kg; view cost history over time. Species Requirement Profiles: view and edit minimum/maximum nutrient bounds per species (broiler starter/grower/finisher, layer, swine backgrounder, beef stocker) and production stage. Ration Formulator: select ingredient subset, set per-ingredient inclusion min/max bounds, run LP solve, view optimal inclusion percentages and total ration cost per tonne, see binding constraint shadow prices. Ration Version History: save named ration versions, compare side-by-side across versions, track cost delta over ingredient price changes. PDF Export: generate specification sheet with inclusion table, nutritional analysis summary, and cost breakdown.
target_output.system_components: Electron main process (Node 20, database access, Python IPC, file system), React renderer process (Vite-bundled SPA, Zustand state, UI components), Python solver subprocess (linprog LP engine, ReportLab PDF generator, SQLAlchemy data layer), SQLCipher SQLite database file (ingredients, rations, inventory, users), NSIS installer (bundles Electron runtime, React build, embedded Python 3.12 with SciPy + ReportLab + SQLAlchemy).
target_output.workflows: Least-Cost Ration Solve: Nutritionist opens Ration Formulator, selects ingredients from library (checkboxes), enters per-ingredient min/max inclusion percentages and the target batch size. Clicks 'Solve'. Electron main process sends {action: 'solve', ingredients: [...], constraints: [...]} JSON to Python subprocess stdin. Python builds the linprog cost vector (cost_per_kg per ingredient), constraint matrix (nutritional profile rows), bounds tuple, and calls linprog(method='highs'). On optimal result, returns {status: 'optimal', inclusion_pct: {...}, cost_per_tonne: 342.50, binding_constraints: ['min_lysine']}. React displays solution table and binding constraint highlight. PDF Export: Nutritionist opens Export panel, selects saved ration version, clicks Generate PDF. Electron sends {action: 'export_pdf', formula_id: 42} to Python. ReportLab generates spec sheet with ingredient table, proximate analysis, and cost summary to /tmp/ration_42.pdf. Electron copies to user-selected save path.
target_output.data_model: feed_ingredients (id INTEGER PK, name TEXT UNIQUE, supplier TEXT, dry_matter_pct REAL, crude_protein_pct REAL, metabolizable_energy_kcal_kg REAL, fat_pct REAL, crude_fiber_pct REAL, lysine_pct REAL, methionine_pct REAL, phosphorus_pct REAL, calcium_pct REAL, created_at TEXT). animal_species_requirements (id INTEGER PK, species TEXT, production_stage TEXT, nutrient_name TEXT, min_value REAL, max_value REAL, unit TEXT). ration_formulas (id INTEGER PK, name TEXT, species TEXT, production_stage TEXT, created_by TEXT, version INTEGER DEFAULT 1, total_cost_per_tonne REAL, created_at TEXT). ration_ingredients (id INTEGER PK, formula_id INTEGER FK, ingredient_id INTEGER FK, inclusion_pct REAL, min_inclusion REAL DEFAULT 0, max_inclusion REAL DEFAULT 1). ingredient_inventory (id INTEGER PK, ingredient_id INTEGER FK, quantity_kg REAL, cost_per_kg REAL, recorded_date TEXT). cost_records (id INTEGER PK, ingredient_id INTEGER FK, cost_per_kg REAL, effective_date TEXT). users (id INTEGER PK, username TEXT UNIQUE, password_hash TEXT, role TEXT CHECK(role IN ('nutritionist','manager')), created_at TEXT).
target_output.api_design: Internal JSON IPC API between Electron and Python subprocess. {action: 'solve', payload: {ingredients: [{id, cost_per_kg, min_pct, max_pct, nutrients: {cp, me, fat, ...}}], constraints: [{nutrient, min, max}]}} -> {status: 'optimal'|'infeasible', inclusion_pct: {ingredient_id: pct}, cost_per_tonne: float, binding_constraints: [nutrient_name]}. {action: 'export_pdf', payload: {formula_id: int, output_path: str}} -> {status: 'ok', path: str}. {action: 'import_ingredients_csv', payload: {csv_path: str}} -> {status: 'ok', imported_count: int, errors: []}. No HTTP endpoints; all IPC is in-process via Electron contextBridge.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SQLCipher AES-256 encryption on the SQLite database file; encryption key derived from user password via PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 with 100,000 iterations and a per-database salt stored in a separate plaintext file at %APPDATA%/FeedMill/db.salt. bcrypt password hashing (cost factor 12) for local user authentication. Session token held in Electron app memory only; never written to disk or renderer process. contextBridge IPC restricts renderer process to exposed API surface; no direct Node.js access from React. No network connections initiated at any point; Electron net module disabled in app config. AAFCO and FDA medicated feed label compliance are business process controls, not software enforcement points.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Electron Builder generates NSIS Windows installer (.exe, ~120MB) bundling Electron 30 runtime, Vite-built React app, Python 3.12 embedded interpreter, and Python packages (SciPy, ReportLab, SQLAlchemy, pysqlcipher3). GitHub Actions CI on windows-latest: (1) pytest runs LP formulator tests against 10 reference ration problems with known optimal solutions; (2) Jest runs React component tests including Ration Formulator solve flow; (3) Electron Builder builds installer artifact. Installer uses NSIS with user-mode install (no admin required for updates). Auto-update via Electron's autoUpdater pointing to a shared network drive or GitHub Releases. Database file location: %APPDATA%/FeedMill/rations.db; backup is user/IT responsibility via standard file backup.
target_output.observability: Local application log file at %APPDATA%/FeedMill/app.log with Winston logger; rolling 30-day file rotation. Log entries for LP solve duration, solution status (optimal/infeasible), constraint violations, PDF export completion, and Python subprocess errors. No external telemetry or error reporting. Support workflow: nutritionist emails log file to developer for debugging. LP solve performance metric logged per solve: ingredient count, constraint count, solve time ms.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero ongoing cost; one-time development effort. Electron app plus embedded Python runtime fits within 200MB installer. SciPy HiGHS solves LP problems with 200 ingredients and 80 nutritional constraints in under 200ms on a modern workstation. SQLite with WAL mode handles concurrent reads from better-sqlite3 and SQLAlchemy without contention. Future ERP integration would require adding a network sync module without redesigning the local-first architecture.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - MVP (12 weeks): Electron + React scaffold, SQLCipher SQLite setup, ingredient library CRUD for 50 common poultry ingredients, broiler and layer species requirement profiles, LP ration formulator with HiGHS backend, basic PDF spec sheet export via ReportLab, local authentication with bcrypt, NSIS installer. Phase 2 - Full Feature Release (8 additional weeks): Swine and cattle species profiles, ingredient CSV bulk import, ration version history with diff comparison, cost history charts, enhanced PDF with nutritional analysis and cost breakdown, auto-update mechanism.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in Electron renderer. Python type hints for all solver functions. pytest coverage must reach 90% for lp_formulator module, validated against 10 reference ration problems with published optimal solutions. contextBridge API surface documented in /src/preload/api.ts. Python subprocess must be gracefully terminated on Electron app quit event. No hardcoded ingredient data; all reference data loaded from seed SQL migrations. SQLAlchemy migrations via Alembic; migration scripts committed to repo.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: pysqlcipher3 Windows build compatibility with embedded Python 3.12. Mitigation: test SQLCipher binding in CI on windows-latest runner before committing to encryption approach; fallback to application-level field encryption if binding fails. Risk: SciPy linprog infeasibility gives no actionable detail beyond binding constraint names. Mitigation: return shadow prices so nutritionist can see how much each binding minimum exceeds available supply. Tradeoff: JSON stdin/stdout IPC over in-process Python (via python-bridge) chosen for process isolation; Python crash does not kill Electron app.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected ReportLab over WeasyPrint for PDF generation due to smaller packaged footprint (no Chromium dependency) and no runtime HTML rendering requirement. Confirmed SciPy HiGHS over GLPK (older PuLP backend) for better performance and active maintenance. SQLCipher chosen over plaintext SQLite because ingredient cost data is commercially sensitive. PBKDF2 key derivation selected over hardcoded encryption key to prevent DB access without valid credentials.

metadata.notes: Feed mill least-cost ration formulator; moderate quality with slightly thin observability section for offline desktop context.

project_goal.value: Research prototype modeling optimal irrigation scheduling for drip-irrigated vineyards using soil moisture sensor data, local weather forecasts, and vine phenology stage to minimize water usage while protecting yield
project_goal.rationale: Core research objective for university viticulture department grant

target_users.value: Viticulture researchers and graduate students in the university vineyard department
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in grant proposal

project_class.rationale: Experimental scheduling model for research validation, not a production irrigation controller

capabilities.rationale: No UI needed; researchers interact via Jupyter notebooks and Grafana dashboards; sensor integration and analytics are core

complexity_level.rationale: Time-series ingestion with scheduling model logic and weather API integration is moderate; no distributed systems or real-time streaming

risk_level.rationale: Research system; incorrect scheduling recommendation affects experimental vineyard blocks, not commercial production

data_sensitivity.rationale: Soil moisture readings and vineyard block data are proprietary research data

external_exposure.rationale: Accessible only on university on-premise Linux server; no public endpoints

access_model.value: Custom JWT for API access by researcher scripts and Jupyter notebooks; service account tokens for sensor data ingestion
access_model.rationale: Programmatic access pattern with no browser UI

feature_scope.value: 15-minute soil moisture sensor ingestion, weather forecast polling from Open-Meteo API, vine phenology stage management, scheduling model (water deficit vs phenology-stage threshold comparison), irrigation event logging, researcher analytics dashboard in Grafana, model drift detection report
feature_scope.rationale: Full scope for grant deliverables

mvp_scope.value: Sensor ingestion pipeline, scheduling model for four phenology stages, irrigation recommendation log, Grafana dashboard for soil moisture and recommendation history
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum needed for first growing season data collection

security_baseline.value: JWT for API access, HTTPS on university network, no PHI or financial data, sensor credentials in environment variables
security_baseline.rationale: Appropriate for internal research prototype

frontend_stack.value: None; Grafana for operational dashboards, Jupyter notebooks for research analysis
frontend_stack.rationale: No custom UI required; Grafana and Jupyter cover all researcher interaction patterns

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with NumPy and pandas for scheduling model computations; FastAPI for REST endpoints
backend_stack.rationale: NumPy/pandas ecosystem standard for agricultural research time-series modeling

data_platform.value: TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL 16 extension) for time-series soil moisture and irrigation event storage
data_platform.rationale: TimescaleDB hypertables optimize 15-minute sensor time-series queries; native time_bucket aggregation for Grafana

hosting_target.value: University on-premise Linux server (Ubuntu 22.04)
hosting_target.rationale: Grant-funded hardware; no cloud budget; data sovereignty on university systems

privacy_retention_policy.value: Sensor readings and model outputs retained for full grant period (4 years) for reproducibility; archived to university cold storage after grant closes
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Research data retention per university policy

future_scope.value: Automated drip irrigation controller integration via Modbus/MQTT; multi-variety yield impact correlation across seasons
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 grant objectives

constraints.value: Must run on university Linux server; no cloud dependencies; Open-Meteo free API tier (10,000 requests/day); GitHub Actions for CI
constraints.rationale: Budget and infrastructure constraints

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards with Prometheus metrics; alert for sensor ingestion failures and scheduling model exceptions
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard open-source monitoring for on-premise research systems

execution_preference.value: Research milestones aligned with vineyard growing seasons (bud break, flowering, veraison, harvest)
execution_preference.rationale: Model validation tied to phenological growth stages

project.goal: Minimize vineyard irrigation water use while maintaining yield-protecting soil water deficit thresholds per phenology stage
project.target_users: Viticulture researchers and graduate students
project.timeline: First growing season data collection by bud break; full model validation by harvest

backend.framework: FastAPI for REST API; APScheduler for 15-minute ingestion cron
backend.modeling: NumPy and pandas for water balance and evapotranspiration calculations

security.auth: Custom JWT; service account tokens for sensor ingest scripts

data.orm: SQLAlchemy 2.0 with asyncpg for async queries

devops.environments: dev and production via Docker Compose

open_questions.et_method: Penman-Monteith vs simplified Hargreaves for evapotranspiration estimation given available weather variables

confirmed_decisions.timeseries_db: TimescaleDB hypertables for soil_moisture_readings and irrigation_events

reasoner_reviews.product: The key research output is a quantified comparison of actual water applied versus model-recommended water over a full growing season, disaggregated by vineyard block and phenology stage. The model drift detection report is essential for the grant deliverable: researchers need to identify when the model systematically over- or under-recommends irrigation relative to measured vine water stress indicators.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: TimescaleDB time_bucket aggregation for hourly and daily soil moisture summaries is the right choice over raw 15-minute query scans in Grafana. The scheduling model should be separated into a stateless function (inputs: current SWD, phenology stage, forecast ET; output: irrigate/skip + recommended duration) so it can be unit-tested independently of the ingestion pipeline. APScheduler running inside FastAPI is sufficient for 15-minute cron jobs at this data volume.
reasoner_reviews.security: JWT service account tokens for sensor ingestion scripts are appropriate. The main gap identified in round 1 was that token rotation for sensor accounts was not addressed; long-lived sensor tokens present a risk if a sensor device is compromised. Recommend 90-day token expiry with a rotation script.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Open-Meteo free tier provides 15-day hourly forecasts sufficient for 7-day scheduling horizon. The 10,000 req/day limit is well above the 96 daily polls (every 15 minutes) needed for hourly forecast updates. Penman-Monteith ET0 calculation requires wind speed data; confirm Open-Meteo provides wind speed at 10m for the vineyard location.

backend.service_design: FastAPI app with three domain modules: ingest (soil moisture sensor polling and storage), scheduler (scheduling model execution every 15 minutes via APScheduler), weather (Open-Meteo forecast polling and ET0 computation)
backend.api_patterns: REST endpoints for researcher access: GET /blocks/:id/recommendations (last N irrigation recommendations), GET /blocks/:id/moisture (time-series query), POST /phenology/:block_id (update current stage), GET /model/drift-report (seasonal comparison)
backend.background_jobs: APScheduler InMemoryJobStore runs three cron jobs: poll_sensors (every 15 min), poll_weather (every 15 min), run_scheduling_model (every 15 min after sensor and weather updates complete)
backend.failure_handling: Sensor polling failure logs to sensor_ingestion_errors table with consecutive_failures counter; alert triggered when count > 3. Weather API failure uses last cached forecast (up to 6 hours stale) before raising alert.

security.auth_design: FastAPI with python-jose JWT; researcher tokens (30-day expiry), sensor service account tokens (90-day expiry with rotation script). Tokens validated in FastAPI dependency injection.
security.secrets_management: Sensor API keys, TimescaleDB connection string in .env on server; excluded from GitHub repo. Token rotation script runs via cron, generates new token, updates sensor device config file.

previous_plan.title: Vineyard Irrigation Scheduling Prototype v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A research prototype ingesting 15-minute soil moisture sensor data and Open-Meteo weather forecasts into TimescaleDB to drive a vine phenology-aware scheduling model that recommends drip irrigation events to minimize water use.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: FastAPI backend with APScheduler for 15-minute cron jobs. TimescaleDB hypertables for soil_moisture_readings and irrigation_events. Open-Meteo API integration for weather forecasts. Grafana dashboards for researcher visualization.
previous_plan.data_model: soil_moisture_readings (HYPERTABLE), irrigation_events, vineyard_blocks, vine_phenology_stages, evapotranspiration_estimates. No ET0 computation method specified.
previous_plan.api_design: GET /blocks/:id/recommendations, GET /blocks/:id/moisture, POST /phenology/:block_id. No drift report endpoint.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision addresses ET0 methodology ambiguity and sensor token security. Specifying FAO-56 Penman-Monteith with Open-Meteo wind speed at 10m (confirmed available) and Hargreaves fallback when wind data is missing resolves the scientific validity risk. Adding 90-day sensor token expiry with a rotation cron script closes the access control gap. Adding GET /model/drift-report with seasonal recommendation vs actual irrigation volume delta addresses the grant deliverable. TimescaleDB and APScheduler architecture from round 1 is retained.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified FAO-56 Penman-Monteith ET0 formula using Open-Meteo fields: temperature_2m, relative_humidity_2m, wind_speed_10m, shortwave_radiation. Confirmed Open-Meteo free tier provides wind_speed_10m for the vineyard latitude. Documented Hargreaves simplified ET0 as fallback when wind speed data is missing. Added ET0 computation step to scheduling workflow. evapotranspiration_estimates now stores method field (penman_monteith or hargreaves).
fix_report.expected_outcome: ET0 estimates are methodologically sound for Penman-Monteith; fallback to Hargreaves maintains continuity when wind speed data gaps occur

fix_report.action_taken: Defined 90-day expiry for sensor service account JWT tokens. Added token rotation cron script that generates new token, writes to sensor config file on server, and logs rotation event. Researcher tokens retain 30-day expiry. Added to security_and_compliance section.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Compromised sensor device token expires within 90 days; rotation script reduces manual re-keying burden

target_output.title: Vineyard Drip Irrigation Scheduling Prototype v2
target_output.executive_summary: A research prototype on Python 3.12, FastAPI, and TimescaleDB that ingests 15-minute soil moisture sensor readings and Open-Meteo weather forecasts to drive a vine phenology-aware irrigation scheduling model minimizing water application in drip-irrigated vineyard blocks. FAO-56 Penman-Monteith ET0 with Hargreaves fallback computes daily evapotranspiration. Grafana dashboards and a REST API provide researcher access to recommendations, water balance trends, and a seasonal model drift report. Runs on university on-premise Linux server.
target_output.architecture_overview: FastAPI application on Ubuntu 22.04 serves researcher REST endpoints and runs APScheduler for three 15-minute cron jobs: sensor polling (reads current volumetric water content from sensor API), weather polling (queries Open-Meteo for 7-day hourly forecast and computes FAO-56 Penman-Monteith ET0 or Hargreaves ET0 when wind speed unavailable), and the scheduling model (compares current soil water deficit against vine phenology-stage threshold and writes irrigation recommendation to irrigation_events). All time-series data lands in TimescaleDB hypertables with 7-day chunks. TimescaleDB continuous aggregates pre-compute hourly and daily soil moisture averages for Grafana panels. Prometheus collects FastAPI and scheduler metrics; Grafana dashboards provide the primary researcher interface.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.111, APScheduler 3.10, NumPy 1.26, pandas 2.2, httpx for Open-Meteo async requests, SQLAlchemy 2.0 with asyncpg for TimescaleDB. Database: TimescaleDB 2.15 (PostgreSQL 16 extension); hypertables with 7-day chunks for soil_moisture_readings and weather_forecasts; continuous aggregates for hourly/daily summaries. Auth: python-jose JWT; researcher (30-day expiry), sensor service account (90-day expiry with rotation cron). Monitoring: Prometheus, Grafana 10 with TimescaleDB data source plugin. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Docker Compose on Ubuntu 22.04.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Sensor Ingestion: 15-minute polling from soil moisture sensors via REST API; volumetric water content stored per block and depth in soil_moisture_readings hypertable. Weather and ET0: 15-minute Open-Meteo forecast refresh; FAO-56 Penman-Monteith ET0 computed from temperature, humidity, wind speed, and radiation; Hargreaves fallback when wind speed unavailable. Scheduling Model: per-block soil water deficit computed from field capacity, current moisture, and cumulative ET0; compared against vine_phenology_stages SWD threshold; irrigation recommendation written if deficit exceeds threshold. Phenology Stage Management: POST /phenology/:block_id to update current growth stage and SWD threshold for active blocks. Drift Report: GET /model/drift-report computes seasonal recommended vs actual irrigation volume per block with percentage deviation. Grafana Dashboards: soil moisture trend, daily ET0 vs precipitation, recommendation history, water savings summary.
target_output.system_components: FastAPI container (REST API, APScheduler cron jobs, JWT auth), TimescaleDB container (PostgreSQL 16 with TimescaleDB extension, hypertables, continuous aggregates), Grafana container (dashboards for researcher visualization), Prometheus container (metrics scraping and alerting), nginx reverse proxy (HTTPS termination for API and Grafana). Open-Meteo external API dependency (free tier, polled every 15 minutes).
target_output.workflows: Scheduling Model Execution (every 15 minutes): APScheduler triggers run_scheduling_model job after sensor and weather polls complete. For each active vineyard_block: (1) query soil_moisture_readings for latest moisture_pct at root zone depth; (2) retrieve current vine_phenology_stages SWD threshold for the block; (3) compute soil water deficit = (field_capacity_pct - current_moisture_pct) * root_zone_depth_mm; (4) retrieve ET0 from evapotranspiration_estimates for current day; (5) compute adjusted deficit including projected ET0 for next 24h; (6) if deficit > swd_threshold, write irrigation_events record with recommended_duration_min = deficit / emitter_rate; (7) if deficit <= threshold, write recommendation with trigger_reason='below_threshold'. Model Drift Report Generation: Researcher calls GET /model/drift-report?block_id=1&season=2026. FastAPI queries irrigation_events for all recommended events; joins with actual_duration_min entries logged by field staff; computes per-block sum of recommended_duration_min vs actual_duration_min and percentage deviation; returns JSON array with block, period, recommended_volume_L, actual_volume_L, deviation_pct.
target_output.data_model: soil_moisture_readings (time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL, block_id INTEGER FK, sensor_id INTEGER FK, moisture_pct REAL, depth_cm INTEGER) HYPERTABLE chunks_7_days. weather_forecasts (time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL, block_id INTEGER FK, temperature_c REAL, humidity_pct REAL, wind_speed_ms REAL, precipitation_mm REAL, shortwave_radiation_wm2 REAL, et0_mm_penman_monteith REAL, et0_mm_hargreaves REAL, et0_method TEXT) HYPERTABLE. evapotranspiration_estimates (id SERIAL PK, block_id INTEGER FK, computed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, et0_mm REAL, kc_coefficient REAL, etc_mm REAL, method TEXT CHECK(method IN ('penman_monteith','hargreaves'))). irrigation_events (id SERIAL PK, block_id INTEGER FK, recommended_at TIMESTAMPTZ, recommended_duration_min INTEGER, actual_duration_min INTEGER, trigger_reason TEXT, swd_deficit_mm REAL, phenology_stage TEXT). vineyard_blocks (id INTEGER PK, block_name TEXT, variety TEXT, area_ha REAL, soil_type TEXT, field_capacity_pct REAL, wilting_point_pct REAL, root_zone_depth_mm INTEGER, emitter_rate_lph REAL). vine_phenology_stages (id INTEGER PK, block_id INTEGER FK, stage TEXT CHECK(stage IN ('bud_break','flowering','fruit_set','veraison','harvest')), start_date DATE, swd_threshold_mm REAL). sensor_devices (id INTEGER PK, block_id INTEGER FK, sensor_model TEXT, depth_cm INTEGER, api_endpoint TEXT, service_token_expires DATE).
target_output.api_design: GET /blocks/:id/recommendations?limit=48 (last 48 irrigation recommendations for a block, ordered by recommended_at DESC). GET /blocks/:id/moisture?from=ISO8601&to=ISO8601 (soil moisture time-series query from TimescaleDB hypertable). POST /phenology/:block_id (body: {stage, start_date, swd_threshold_mm}, updates active phenology stage). GET /model/drift-report?block_id=int&season=YYYY (seasonal recommended vs actual volume comparison). GET /et0/:block_id/today (current ET0 estimate with method flag). GET /health (liveness probe for monitoring). All endpoints require Bearer JWT in Authorization header.
target_output.security_and_compliance: python-jose JWT with two token profiles: researcher tokens (30-day expiry, RS256 signed), sensor service account tokens (90-day expiry, RS256 signed, scope restricted to POST /ingest endpoints). Token rotation script runs monthly via cron: generates new sensor token, writes to /etc/sensor-agent/token on server, logs rotation event to audit_log table. HTTPS enforced by Nginx with university CA certificate; API and Grafana both behind Nginx. Sensor API keys (Open-Meteo does not require keys on free tier) and TimescaleDB connection string in .env on server; excluded from GitHub repo. State water use reporting for agricultural permits is a researcher reporting obligation, not a software control.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Docker Compose on Ubuntu 22.04 with named volumes for timescaledb_data and grafana_data. GitHub Actions pipeline: (1) pytest unit tests for Penman-Monteith ET0 computation (validated against FAO-56 reference values), Hargreaves ET0, SWD calculation, and scheduling model decision logic; (2) flake8 linting; (3) Docker build; (4) SSH deploy job: docker compose pull && docker compose up -d on university server. Environments: dev (port 8001 FastAPI, port 3001 Grafana) and production (port 443 via Nginx) on same server with separate Docker Compose networks. Database migrations via Alembic; TimescaleDB hypertable creation and continuous aggregate setup run as migration steps. Manual rollback via docker compose rollback to previous image tag.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes FastAPI /metrics (HTTP request count and latency by endpoint), APScheduler job execution count and duration by job name, and PostgreSQL pg_stat_activity via postgres_exporter. Grafana dashboards: Soil Moisture Trend per Block (15-minute readings with field capacity and wilting point reference lines), Daily ET0 vs Precipitation per Block, Irrigation Recommendation History (recommended vs actual duration per event), Scheduling Model Decision Rate (irrigate vs skip ratio by phenology stage), Water Savings Summary (seasonal actual vs model-recommended volume). Prometheus alert rules: sensor_ingestion_consecutive_failures > 3, scheduling_model_job_failure, TimescaleDB disk usage > 80% of allocated volume.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost; all compute on university on-premise server. Open-Meteo free tier provides 10,000 req/day; 15-minute polling for 5 blocks requires 480 req/day (well within limit). TimescaleDB storage at 15-minute resolution: approximately 500MB per sensor per growing season (180 days); 10 sensors across 5 blocks equals ~5GB per season. Continuous aggregates reduce Grafana query scan time from full hypertable to pre-computed chunks. No horizontal scaling needed for research-scale deployment.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Ingestion and MVP Scheduling (8 weeks, pre-bud break): Soil moisture sensor ingestion pipeline, Open-Meteo weather polling, Penman-Monteith ET0 computation, scheduling model for 4 phenology stages, irrigation_events logging, basic Grafana dashboard, JWT auth. Phase 2 - Research Analytics (6 weeks, mid-season): Model drift detection report endpoint, TimescaleDB continuous aggregates for daily water balance, enhanced Grafana panels with water savings summary, sensor token rotation script, Hargreaves ET0 fallback. Phase 3 - Multi-Season Comparison (deferred to growing season 2): Multi-year vineyard block comparison dashboards, yield correlation data ingestion, automated grant reporting CSV export.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python type hints required for all FastAPI route functions and APScheduler job definitions. pytest coverage must reach 90% for ET0 computation functions (Penman-Monteith and Hargreaves) validated against FAO-56 reference dataset. TimescaleDB hypertable creation and continuous aggregate DDL committed as Alembic migration steps, not raw SQL scripts. Open-Meteo API client wrapped in a retry decorator (httpx with exponential backoff, max 3 retries). NumPy array operations for ET0 computation over pandas Series for performance at 15-minute aggregation scale. GitLab MR (or GitHub PR) requires one senior researcher approval.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Open-Meteo free tier API returns wind speed gaps for the vineyard location; Hargreaves fallback introduces ~10% ET0 estimation error versus Penman-Monteith. Mitigation: log et0_method per estimate; report fallback frequency in Grafana dashboard. Risk: APScheduler InMemoryJobStore loses scheduled jobs on FastAPI restart; jobs resume after restart but may miss one 15-minute cycle. Mitigation: acceptable for research context; alert on missed ingestion via sensor_ingestion_consecutive_failures > 3. Tradeoff: Docker Compose single-host over Kubernetes selected for simplicity given research-scale data volumes and no redundancy requirement.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed FAO-56 Penman-Monteith ET0 as primary method using Open-Meteo wind_speed_10m field available in the free tier hourly forecast. Hargreaves simplified ET0 implemented as fallback requiring only temperature and extraterrestrial radiation (no wind speed). TimescaleDB continuous aggregates chosen over materialized views for native time_bucket refresh integration with Grafana. Sensor service account tokens set to 90-day expiry with monthly cron rotation script.

metadata.notes: Vineyard irrigation scheduling research prototype; round 2 resolves ET0 method ambiguity and sensor token expiry; no frontend specialist subplan as capabilities exclude frontend.

project_goal.value: Public-facing landing page for a soil testing laboratory displaying a live dashboard of regional average nutrient levels aggregated from recent soil samples, prompting farmers to submit their own samples for county-level comparison
project_goal.rationale: Marketing and community engagement tool driving sample submission leads

target_users.value: Farmers and agricultural producers seeking county-level soil nutrient benchmarks
target_users.rationale: Primary audience identified in lab marketing strategy

project_class.rationale: Static site with weekly-updated data feed; no server-side runtime or database required

capabilities.rationale: Static frontend with CDN-hosted data feed and Vercel Analytics; no auth, backend, or payments

complexity_level.rationale: Static site with client-side data fetch from CDN JSON; no server-side processing

risk_level.rationale: Public-facing with no user accounts, no PII collection, and no financial transactions

data_sensitivity.rationale: All displayed data is aggregated county-level averages; no individual farmer data or PII published

external_exposure.rationale: Public marketing site accessible to any farmer without authentication

access_model.value: No authentication; fully public read access; sample submission form collects name and contact info sent via Formspree
access_model.rationale: Public marketing landing page with no restricted content

feature_scope.value: Regional nutrient dashboard (county-level averages for pH, P, K, Ca, Mg, Zn), interactive county map widget, lab service package descriptions, sample submission request form, nutrient trend indicator (vs. prior season average)
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature scope agreed with lab marketing team

mvp_scope.value: County nutrient dashboard with current-season averages, county selection map, sample submission CTA button linking to Formspree form
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum needed to launch before spring soil sampling season

security_baseline.value: HTTPS via Vercel; Content-Security-Policy headers; no user credentials or PII stored on site
security_baseline.rationale: Standard static site security; no backend attack surface

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with Tailwind CSS; static export (next export); Chart.js for nutrient bar charts; Mapbox GL JS free tier for county map
frontend_stack.rationale: Next.js static export gives SEO-friendly HTML with React component model; Mapbox GL JS handles county GeoJSON overlays

backend_stack.value: None; weekly JSON data feed published to Vercel Blob or CDN by lab's internal system
backend_stack.rationale: No server-side runtime; data feed published from existing lab LIMS system on a weekly schedule

data_platform.value: None; weekly-published static JSON feed at /data/county-nutrients.json served from Vercel CDN
data_platform.rationale: No database required; data is pre-aggregated and published as a static asset

hosting_target.rationale: Free tier supports the expected traffic; instant preview deployments for review

privacy_retention_policy.value: No user data collected or stored on site; Formspree handles submission contact data per their privacy policy; Vercel Analytics is cookieless
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No personal data processing by the lab site

future_scope.value: Interactive multi-year nutrient trend charts; farmer login for personalized sample result comparison against county averages
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 features contingent on LIMS API availability for real-time sample results

constraints.value: Must use Vercel free tier; Mapbox GL JS free tier (50,000 map loads/month); no server-side runtime; weekly data feed update cadence
constraints.rationale: Zero-budget static site; lab has no cloud hosting budget

observability_baseline.value: Vercel Analytics for page views and Core Web Vitals; no custom instrumentation required
observability_baseline.rationale: Vercel Analytics dashboard is sufficient for a landing page with no backend

execution_preference.value: Single sprint delivery ahead of spring soil sampling season
execution_preference.rationale: Time-sensitive launch aligned with seasonal farmer demand

project.goal: Static landing page displaying county-level soil nutrient benchmarks to drive sample submission leads
project.target_users: Farmers and agricultural producers
project.timeline: Launch before spring soil sampling season

frontend.mapping: Mapbox GL JS free tier for county selection map

security.headers: Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options via Vercel headers config

data.feed: Static JSON at /data/county-nutrients.json, fetched client-side on page load

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for build and lint

constraints.maps: Mapbox GL JS free tier 50K loads/month

open_questions.map_interaction: Click-to-select county vs dropdown selector for county navigation

confirmed_decisions.static_export: next export to generate fully static HTML/JS/CSS

reasoner_reviews.product: The core conversion action is the sample submission CTA. County map interactivity (click-to-select county) increases engagement by letting farmers directly identify their location before seeing comparative nutrient averages. The nutrient trend indicator versus prior season is a strong differentiator that communicates lab data depth to potential customers.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Next.js 14 static export is correct for a zero-runtime landing page. The county-nutrients.json data feed should include both current-season averages and prior-season averages per county so the trend delta can be computed client-side without a second fetch. Chart.js renders nutrient bar charts performantly at this scale. Mapbox GL JS free tier is adequate for a county selection map with static GeoJSON boundaries.
reasoner_reviews.security: No backend attack surface. CSP headers should restrict script-src to self and cdn.jsdelivr.net for Chart.js and api.mapbox.com for Mapbox GL. Formspree handles submission data outside the site's trust boundary. Cookieless Vercel Analytics avoids GDPR cookie consent banner requirement.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Vercel free tier supports 100GB bandwidth and 100 deployments/month, well above expected traffic for a regional lab landing page. Mapbox GL JS 50K map loads/month is sufficient unless the page goes viral during planting season; fallback is a static SVG county map.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 static export with three pages: index (hero, nutrient dashboard, county map, CTA), about (lab services and service packages), and contact (Formspree submission form); no API routes
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home page: hero section with lab brand, CountyNutrientDashboard component (fetches /data/county-nutrients.json on mount, renders Chart.js bar charts for selected county), CountyMapWidget (Mapbox GL JS with county GeoJSON boundaries, click handler updates selected county state), LabServicePackages section, SampleSubmissionCTA button. Form page: Formspree-powered sample request form with county, crop type, and contact fields.
frontend.performance_notes: next export generates fully static HTML; county-nutrients.json prefetched via <link rel=preload>; Chart.js loaded asynchronously after initial paint; Mapbox GL JS lazy-loaded on map viewport entry; Tailwind CSS purged to <10KB
frontend.accessibility_notes: Nutrient bar charts include aria-label descriptions; county map has keyboard-navigable county list as fallback; color scales use Colorbrewer palette with sufficient contrast for color-blind users
frontend.frontend_security_notes: CSP headers in vercel.json: script-src 'self' cdn.jsdelivr.net api.mapbox.com events.mapbox.com; no inline scripts; X-Frame-Options DENY; Referrer-Policy same-origin

security.auth_design: No authentication; fully public site
security.abuse_prevention: Formspree handles submission rate limiting and spam filtering; no server-side form processing on the site. Mapbox public token restricted to the lab domain via Mapbox token scoping.

data.entities: county_nutrient_averages (county_fips TEXT, county_name TEXT, state TEXT, season TEXT, ph_avg REAL, phosphorus_ppm_avg REAL, potassium_ppm_avg REAL, calcium_ppm_avg REAL, magnesium_ppm_avg REAL, zinc_ppm_avg REAL, sample_count INTEGER, prior_season_ph_avg REAL, prior_season_phosphorus_ppm_avg REAL). Delivered as a static JSON array in /public/data/county-nutrients.json. Also: nutrient_categories (nutrient_name, unit, low_threshold, optimal_min, optimal_max, description) embedded as a static JS constant. lab_service_packages (name, description, turnaround_days, price_range) embedded in Next.js page component.
data.analytics_events: Vercel Analytics automatic page view tracking; client-side custom event for county_selected (county_fips) and cta_click (service_package) via Vercel Analytics track() API

devops.deployment_topology: Vercel Edge Network: automatic CDN distribution of static HTML/JS/CSS build output; county-nutrients.json served from Vercel public directory as static asset
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint + TypeScript type check, next build with static export (next export), Vercel preview deployment on PR via Vercel GitHub integration. Production deployment triggered by merge to main.
devops.environments: Vercel preview deployments per PR branch; production deployment at lab domain on merge to main

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a fully static Next.js landing page with no server-side runtime. The county-nutrients.json data feed is the sole dynamic element, fetched client-side from Vercel CDN. Key decisions: include prior-season averages in the JSON feed to enable client-side trend delta computation without a second fetch; use Mapbox GL JS for county map with token scoped to lab domain; Chart.js loaded asynchronously to preserve Core Web Vitals LCP score.
target_output.title: Soil Testing Lab Regional Nutrient Dashboard Landing Page
target_output.executive_summary: A fully static Next.js 14 landing page for a soil testing laboratory that displays county-level soil nutrient averages (pH, P, K, Ca, Mg, Zn) aggregated from recent samples, served from Vercel Edge Network. Farmers select their county on an interactive Mapbox GL JS map to view comparative nutrient levels and trend indicators versus the prior season. A sample submission CTA drives lead generation via Formspree. The weekly-updated county-nutrients.json data feed is published by the lab's existing LIMS system as a static CDN asset.
target_output.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 static export generates fully pre-rendered HTML pages at build time, deployed to Vercel Edge Network for global CDN delivery. No server-side API routes or database. The county-nutrients.json data feed at /data/county-nutrients.json is a static public asset updated weekly by the lab's internal LIMS system (outside this project's scope) and served from Vercel CDN with long cache TTL. On page load, the CountyNutrientDashboard React component fetches the JSON feed, populates a county lookup map, and renders Chart.js bar charts for the farmer-selected county. Mapbox GL JS renders county boundary GeoJSON overlaid on a satellite basemap; county click events update the selected county React state. Vercel Analytics provides cookieless page view and interaction tracking without a cookie consent banner.
target_output.technology_stack: Framework: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, static export (output: export in next.config.js). Styling: Tailwind CSS 3.4 (purged build). Charts: Chart.js 4.4 loaded asynchronously via dynamic import. Maps: Mapbox GL JS 3.0 with county boundary GeoJSON (US Census TIGER simplified to 500k resolution). Form: Formspree React library for sample submission. Analytics: Vercel Analytics with track() API for custom county_selected and cta_click events. Hosting: Vercel Edge Network, automatic HTTPS, preview deployments. CI: GitHub Actions (ESLint, TypeScript tsc --noEmit, next build).
target_output.functional_feature_map: County Nutrient Dashboard: client-side fetch of /data/county-nutrients.json on mount; Chart.js grouped bar chart rendering pH, P, K, Ca, Mg, Zn averages for selected county with prior-season comparison bars; trend delta badges (up/down arrow + percent change). County Map Widget: Mapbox GL JS map with TIGER county GeoJSON boundaries; counties shaded by composite nutrient index; click-to-select county updates dashboard state; keyboard-navigable county dropdown fallback. Nutrient Interpretation Guide: static informational section with optimal range indicators per nutrient. Lab Service Packages: static cards for Basic Panel, Comprehensive Panel, and Micronutrient Panel with turnaround times and pricing tiers. Sample Submission CTA: Formspree React form collecting county, crop type, acreage, name, and email; success state with expected turnaround confirmation.
target_output.system_components: Vercel Edge Network (CDN for static HTML/JS/CSS and county-nutrients.json), Next.js 14 static export build (pre-rendered HTML pages), CountyNutrientDashboard React component (Chart.js charts, JSON feed consumer), CountyMapWidget React component (Mapbox GL JS, county GeoJSON layer), Formspree endpoint (external service for submission handling), Vercel Analytics (cookieless page view and event tracking), GitHub Actions CI (lint, type check, build verification).
target_output.workflows: Nutrient Dashboard Interaction: Farmer loads the page; browser fetches /data/county-nutrients.json (served from Vercel CDN, cached for 24 hours with Cache-Control: public, max-age=86400, stale-while-revalidate=604800). CountyNutrientDashboard parses JSON into a Map keyed by county_fips. Default county pre-selected from browser geolocation API (fallback: first county in dataset). Chart.js bar chart renders current-season averages with prior_season averages as comparison series; trend delta badges computed as (current - prior) / prior * 100. When farmer clicks county on Mapbox map, setSelectedCounty updates chart data in <200ms. Sample Submission Flow: Farmer clicks 'Submit Soil Samples' CTA, fills Formspree React form with county, crop type, and contact info, submits. Formspree handles POST to their API, returns success. React form displays confirmation with expected turnaround days from lab_service_packages lookup.
target_output.data_model: county_nutrient_averages: static JSON array delivered in /public/data/county-nutrients.json; each entry: {county_fips, county_name, state, season, sample_count, ph_avg, ph_prior, phosphorus_ppm_avg, phosphorus_ppm_prior, potassium_ppm_avg, potassium_ppm_prior, calcium_ppm_avg, calcium_ppm_prior, magnesium_ppm_avg, magnesium_ppm_prior, zinc_ppm_avg, zinc_ppm_prior}. nutrient_categories: TypeScript constant with {nutrient_name, unit, low_threshold, optimal_min, optimal_max, high_threshold, description} for chart annotation rendering. lab_service_packages: TypeScript constant with {name, panel_nutrients[], turnaround_days, price_display} for service cards. No database; no server-side data persistence.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Client-side data access: GET /data/county-nutrients.json (static Vercel asset, Cache-Control: public max-age=86400 stale-while-revalidate=604800, returns JSON array of county nutrient averages). Formspree form submission: POST https://formspree.io/f/{form_id} (external service, not owned by this project). Mapbox GL JS: GET https://api.mapbox.com/styles/v1/mapbox/satellite-v9/tiles (tile requests via Mapbox public token scoped to lab domain). No Next.js API routes defined.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Vercel HTTPS with automatic TLS certificate renewal. Content-Security-Policy in vercel.json headers config: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' cdn.jsdelivr.net api.mapbox.com events.mapbox.com; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' api.mapbox.com; img-src 'self' data: blob: *.mapbox.com; connect-src 'self' api.mapbox.com events.mapbox.com formspree.io. X-Frame-Options: DENY. Referrer-Policy: same-origin. Mapbox public token restricted to the lab domain via Mapbox token scoping settings (allowed URLs: lab domain only). No user PII collected or stored by the site; Formspree processes contact data under their own privacy policy. Vercel Analytics is cookieless and does not require GDPR cookie consent banner.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Vercel GitHub integration: push to any branch creates a preview deployment with a unique URL for stakeholder review; push to main triggers production deployment to lab domain. GitHub Actions CI runs on every PR: ESLint for TypeScript React code, tsc --noEmit type check, next build with output: export to verify static generation succeeds. Build artifact is /out directory of pre-rendered HTML. No server configuration required; Vercel manages CDN, HTTPS, and edge delivery automatically. Weekly data feed update: lab's LIMS system uploads new county-nutrients.json to Vercel Blob storage, triggering a Vercel revalidation webhook to refresh CDN cache. vercel.json configures headers (CSP, X-Frame-Options) and redirects (HTTP to HTTPS).
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics dashboard provides page views, unique visitors, and Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS) per deployment. Custom events tracked via Vercel Analytics track(): county_selected (county_fips property) for understanding geographic engagement, cta_click (package_name property) for conversion attribution. No custom Prometheus or Grafana; Vercel Analytics is sufficient for a landing page. Monitor: Mapbox GL JS monthly map load count in Mapbox dashboard to verify free tier compliance (< 50,000 loads/month).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost on Vercel free tier (100GB bandwidth/month; expected traffic <1GB/month for a regional lab). Mapbox GL JS free tier: 50,000 map loads/month; monitor in Mapbox dashboard; fallback to static SVG county map if load count approaches limit. Formspree free tier: 50 submissions/month; upgrade to Formspree Basic ($10/month) if submission volume exceeds free tier during peak spring sampling season. county-nutrients.json file size: approximately 50KB for 3,000 US counties; well within Vercel asset size limits.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - MVP Launch (3 weeks, pre-spring sampling): Next.js 14 static export scaffold, county-nutrients.json data feed schema defined with LIMS team, CountyNutrientDashboard with Chart.js bar charts, county dropdown selector, lab service packages section, Formspree sample submission form, Vercel deployment with CSP headers. Phase 2 - Map and Trend Indicators (2 weeks): Mapbox GL JS county map widget replacing dropdown, trend delta badges vs prior season, nutrient interpretation guide with optimal range annotations, Vercel Analytics custom event tracking. Phase 3 - Future (deferred): Multi-season trend charts, farmer login for personalized sample comparison (requires backend redesign beyond landing_page scope).
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in Next.js. No server-side API routes; treat any attempt to add API routes as an architecture change requiring review. county-nutrients.json schema validated at build time via Zod schema check in next.config.js build step. Chart.js and Mapbox GL JS loaded via Next.js dynamic() with ssr: false to prevent SSR hydration errors with browser-only APIs. Tailwind CSS with consistent nutrient color palette constants in tailwind.config.js. Component tests with React Testing Library for CountyNutrientDashboard county selection logic.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Mapbox GL JS free tier map load limit (50,000/month) reached if the page gains regional media coverage. Mitigation: static SVG county map fallback pre-built as an alternate component; swap via feature flag in county-nutrients.json metadata. Risk: county-nutrients.json weekly update cadence means dashboard shows up to 7-day-old data; displayed as 'Last updated: [date]' badge parsed from JSON feed metadata field. Tradeoff: client-side JSON fetch over Next.js getStaticProps build-time data injection chosen to enable weekly updates without a full site rebuild triggered by the lab LIMS system.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Click-to-select county map chosen over dropdown selector for higher engagement; dropdown retained as keyboard-accessible fallback. Prior-season averages included directly in county-nutrients.json feed (alongside current-season) to enable trend delta computation in a single fetch. Mapbox GL JS selected over Leaflet.js for built-in satellite basemap tile support without requiring a separate tile server.

metadata.notes: Soil testing lab landing page; static-only architecture consistent with landing_page class; no server-side runtime or database as required by spec.
