project.goal: CLI tool for grain break-even price computation from CME futures CSV and farm cost YAML
project.target_users: Farm financial managers

backend.framework: Click for argument parsing, pandas for CSV, PyYAML for cost loading, Rich for terminal output

security.input_validation: Validate CSV and YAML file paths; reject paths outside working directory

data.input: CME Group futures CSV export and farm cost-of-production YAML
data.output: stdout Rich table and optional timestamped CSV

devops.packaging: pyproject.toml with setuptools; installable via pip or pipx

open_questions.csv_schema: CME CSV column names vary by export format (settlement vs end-of-day); need to handle both

reasoner_reviews.product: The break-even selling price computation is straightforward: break_even = (variable_cost_per_bu + fixed_cost_per_bu) / (1 - basis_adjustment). The output table should show contract month, futures settle price, break-even price, and margin per bushel side by side so the financial manager can immediately identify which contract months are above break-even.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Click with a single main command and optional --output flag is the right CLI structure. pandas read_csv handles CME CSV column variations with usecols parameter. The cost YAML schema should be validated via a Pydantic model before computation to give clear error messages for missing cost fields rather than KeyError tracebacks.
reasoner_reviews.security: File path validation via pathlib.Path.resolve() and checking the result is within the current working directory is sufficient. No network connections. PyYAML safe_load prevents YAML deserialization attacks.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Click, pandas, PyYAML, Rich, and Pydantic are all pure Python packages installable via pip with no C extension dependencies except for pandas NumPy, which is pre-built for Python 3.12 on Windows/macOS/Linux.

backend.service_design: Single Python package with four modules: cli (Click command definitions and entry point), parser (CME CSV parsing and schema normalization), calculator (break-even price computation), formatter (Rich table and CSV output rendering)
backend.api_patterns: CLI commands: grain-calc compute --futures path/to/cme.csv --costs path/to/costs.yaml [--output path/to/results.csv] [--commodity corn|soybeans] [--year 2026]. Returns exit code 0 on success, 1 on input validation error, 2 on computation error.
backend.business_modules: CmeFuturesParser: reads CSV with configurable column mapping (settle price, contract month, volume); CostProfileLoader: loads and validates YAML cost structure via Pydantic; BreakEvenCalculator: computes break_even_price = total_cost_per_bu / yield_target; ResultFormatter: renders Rich Table and writes optional CSV
backend.failure_handling: Click exception handlers print user-friendly error messages to stderr for missing files, invalid CSV schema, and YAML validation errors; no Python tracebacks shown to end user

security.auth_design: No authentication; local CLI
security.abuse_prevention: pathlib.Path.resolve() check ensures input file paths resolve to within the current working directory or an explicit --base-dir; PyYAML safe_load prevents YAML code execution

data.entities: futures_price_records: parsed from CME CSV; fields: contract_month (e.g. 'ZCN26'), commodity TEXT, settle_price_usd FLOAT, contract_size_bu INTEGER, trade_date DATE. production_cost_inputs: loaded from YAML; fields: commodity TEXT, variable_cost_per_bu FLOAT (seed, fertilizer, chemical, fuel, labor), fixed_cost_per_bu FLOAT (land, equipment, overhead), yield_target_bu_acre INTEGER, basis_adjustment_usd FLOAT. break_even_calculations: in-memory result; fields: contract_month TEXT, futures_settle FLOAT, break_even_price FLOAT, margin_per_bu FLOAT, above_break_even BOOL. Output as Rich terminal table and optional timestamped CSV.
data.schema_notes: CME end-of-day CSV uses columns Contract, Settle, Volume; CME settlement CSV uses columns Product Description, Contract Month, Settlement Price; parser normalizes both via column mapping config

devops.deployment_topology: Python package installable via pip install grain-calc or pipx install grain-calc; entry point defined in pyproject.toml [project.scripts] as grain-calc = grain_calc.cli:main
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest with 10 reference break-even test cases (corn and soybean), flake8 linting, mypy type checking, build wheel via python -m build, upload to PyPI on version tag
devops.environments: Development: python -m grain_calc.cli compute ... from repo root. Production: installed via pip/pipx.

target_output.thinking_summary: Simple CLI tool for grain break-even computation. Key decisions: Click for argument parsing with clear --futures, --costs, --output, and --commodity flags; pandas for CME CSV normalization with configurable column mapping to handle both end-of-day and settlement CSV formats; Pydantic for cost YAML validation giving clear error messages. No database, no network, no auth. Weak plan quality means some sections are thin.
target_output.title: Grain Break-Even Price Calculator CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 command-line tool that parses CME Group commodity futures price CSV exports and computes break-even selling prices for corn and soybeans from farm-specific cost-of-production YAML inputs. Outputs a Rich-formatted terminal table of contract month, futures settle price, break-even price, and margin per bushel. Optionally writes timestamped CSV output for marketing year price target tracking.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Python package with four modules: cli (Click entry point and command definitions), parser (CME CSV ingestion with two-format column mapping), calculator (break-even arithmetic using cost YAML inputs), and formatter (Rich Table rendering and CSV writer). The cli module orchestrates: parse CSV -> load and validate cost YAML -> compute break-even per contract month -> format and output. All execution is synchronous and in-process; no subprocesses, threads, or network connections. Installed via pip or pipx as a console_scripts entry point.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12. Click 8.1 for CLI argument parsing and flag definitions. pandas 2.2 for CME CSV parsing with dtype specification and column normalization. PyYAML 6.0 with safe_load for cost profile input. Pydantic 2.6 for cost YAML schema validation and error messaging. Rich 13.7 for terminal table formatting with colored margin indicators. pyproject.toml with setuptools for packaging. pytest for unit tests. mypy for static type checking. GitHub Actions for CI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: compute command: primary command accepting --futures (path to CME CSV), --costs (path to cost YAML), --commodity (corn or soybeans), --year (contract year filter), optional --output (path for CSV write). Outputs: Rich Table with columns Contract Month, Settle Price, Break-Even, Margin/Bu, Status (Above/Below). validate-costs command: loads and validates a cost YAML file, prints parsed cost profile to confirm correct input before running compute. version command: prints package version.
target_output.system_components: grain_calc.cli (Click command group, argument parsing, entry point), grain_calc.parser (CmeFuturesParser class handling end-of-day and settlement CSV column schemas), grain_calc.calculator (BreakEvenCalculator with break_even_price computation), grain_calc.formatter (RichTableFormatter and CsvExporter), grain_calc.models (Pydantic models: CostProfile, FuturesRecord, BreakEvenResult), pyproject.toml (package metadata and console_scripts entry point).
target_output.workflows: Break-Even Compute: User runs grain-calc compute --futures cme_corn_eod.csv --costs corn_costs.yaml --commodity corn --year 2026. CLI validates file paths via pathlib.Path.resolve(). CmeFuturesParser detects CSV format (end-of-day vs settlement) by checking for 'Settle' vs 'Settlement Price' column headers; normalizes to {contract_month, settle_price, commodity, trade_date} records; filters to requested commodity and year. CostProfileLoader reads YAML with PyYAML safe_load, passes dict to CostProfile Pydantic model for validation; raises ClickException with field-level message on validation error. BreakEvenCalculator iterates futures records: break_even = (variable_cost_per_bu + fixed_cost_per_bu); margin = settle_price - break_even - basis_adjustment. RichTableFormatter builds Rich Table with green margin rows (margin > 0) and red rows (margin < 0). Optional CSV write: if --output provided, CsvExporter writes {contract_month, settle_price, break_even_price, margin_per_bu, above_break_even, computed_at} with ISO8601 timestamp in filename.
target_output.data_model: futures_price_records (in-memory list): contract_month TEXT (e.g. ZCN26), commodity TEXT, settle_price_usd FLOAT, contract_size_bu INTEGER, trade_date DATE - parsed from CME CSV by CmeFuturesParser. production_cost_inputs (Pydantic model from YAML): commodity TEXT, variable_cost_per_bu FLOAT (sum of seed, fertilizer, chemical, fuel, harvest_labor), fixed_cost_per_bu FLOAT (land_charge, equipment_depreciation, overhead), yield_target_bu_acre INTEGER, basis_adjustment_usd FLOAT. break_even_calculations (in-memory): contract_month TEXT, futures_settle FLOAT, break_even_price FLOAT, margin_per_bu FLOAT, above_break_even BOOL. Output CSV: same fields plus computed_at TIMESTAMPTZ for tracking over marketing year.
target_output.api_design: CLI command interface (no HTTP API). grain-calc compute --futures FILE --costs FILE [--commodity corn|soybeans] [--year INT] [--output FILE]: reads CME CSV and cost YAML, prints Rich Table to stdout, optionally writes CSV. grain-calc validate-costs --costs FILE: validates YAML structure and prints parsed CostProfile fields to stdout. grain-calc --version: prints version string. Exit codes: 0 success, 1 input validation error (bad file path, invalid YAML schema, unrecognized CSV format), 2 computation error.
target_output.security_and_compliance: File path validation using pathlib.Path.resolve() to prevent path traversal; reject paths that resolve outside current working directory unless --allow-absolute flag is explicitly passed. PyYAML safe_load prevents YAML deserialization code execution. No network connections initiated. No credentials or API keys stored. Input CSV size capped at 10MB via file size check before pandas read_csv to prevent memory exhaustion from malformed input files.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: pyproject.toml defines package metadata, Python 3.12 requirement, dependencies (click, pandas, pyyaml, pydantic, rich), and console_scripts entry point grain-calc = grain_calc.cli:main. Installation: pip install grain-calc or pipx install grain-calc. GitHub Actions CI: pytest with reference test fixtures for CME corn end-of-day CSV and settlement CSV formats, mypy strict type checking, flake8 linting. Release: python -m build produces wheel; twine upload to PyPI on version tag push.
target_output.observability: No logging infrastructure. Error messages printed to stderr via Click's echo(err=True). Click exit codes (0/1/2) enable scripting in shell pipelines. No metrics or telemetry. CSV parse warnings (unrecognised commodity codes, missing close prices) are accumulated and printed as a summary block at command completion rather than interspersed with results, keeping output clean for shell pipeline consumers. A --log-file flag optionally writes the warning summary to a file for audit trail purposes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost; runs locally. pandas CSV parsing handles files up to 10MB (thousands of contract rows) in under 1 second on a modern workstation. No scaling considerations for a single-user local CLI.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - MVP (2 weeks): Click CLI scaffold, CME CSV parser with end-of-day column schema, CostProfile Pydantic model, break-even calculation, Rich Table output, pytest test suite with reference fixtures, pyproject.toml packaging. Phase 2 - Polish (1 week): Settlement CSV format support, --output CSV writer with timestamped filename, validate-costs command, mypy type checking in CI, PyPI publish workflow.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python type hints on all functions. mypy strict mode in CI. pytest fixtures for two CME CSV formats (end-of-day and settlement) and two cost YAML profiles (corn and soybeans). No hardcoded column names; CSV column mapping defined in a config dict in parser module. Rich Table color coding for above/below break-even rows. README with usage examples and sample YAML cost template.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CME CSV column names change with CME export format updates. Mitigation: column mapping dict in parser module is externalized to a config so users can override without code changes. Risk: pandas adds ~50MB to installed package size. Mitigation: acceptable for a local workstation tool; no size constraint on local install. Tradeoff: Pydantic over manual dict validation adds a dependency but gives much clearer YAML error messages for non-technical farm managers.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Click selected over argparse for built-in help text generation and subcommand support. pandas selected over csv module for robust handling of CME CSV quoting and dtype inference edge cases. Pydantic v2 selected over marshmallow for faster validation and better error message formatting.

metadata.notes: Grain break-even CLI tool; weak plan quality per manifest; no FrontendAgent subplan as cli_tool class excludes frontend specialist; observability and cost sections are thin by design.

project_goal.value: Static reference website for agricultural extension educators providing county-level crop production statistics, historical yield tables, and pest pressure maps pre-rendered from USDA NASS datasets for a single US state
project_goal.rationale: Core mission is disseminating publicly available agricultural data to educators with no login or backend required

target_users.value: Agricultural extension educators and county agents across a single US state seeking crop yield references and pest pressure data
target_users.rationale: Primary audience identified as educators needing printable fact sheets and yield trend data

project_class.rationale: No server-side runtime needed; all data pre-rendered at build time from public USDA datasets

capabilities.rationale: Static site with embedded charts and Netlify Analytics; no auth, backend, or database required

complexity_level.rationale: Static HTML generation with data pipeline script; no real-time or interactive backend logic

risk_level.rationale: Public read-only content with no user data collection or financial transactions

data_sensitivity.rationale: All content sourced from public USDA NASS datasets; no personal or sensitive data collected

external_exposure.rationale: Open access reference resource for the extension community statewide

access_model.value: Public read-only, no authentication required
access_model.rationale: Extension reference content must be freely accessible to educators without login friction

feature_scope.value: County-level crop production statistics pages, historical yield trend tables, pest pressure maps, printable fact sheets, crop and county search via Pagefind index
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set identified for extension educator workflows

mvp_scope.value: Static pages for top 10 crops by county with 10-year yield tables and pest pressure ratings; build-time data pipeline from USDA NASS CSV exports; weekly scheduled rebuild
mvp_scope.rationale: Delivers immediate value to educators while full GIS pest mapping deferred to Phase 3

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via Netlify, CSP headers in netlify.toml blocking inline scripts, no user input forms beyond client-side Pagefind search
security_baseline.rationale: Static site with no backend reduces attack surface; HTTPS and CSP cover baseline requirements

frontend_stack.value: Astro 4 with TypeScript content collections, Chart.js 4 for yield trend charts via Astro islands, Tailwind CSS v3 for styling
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro static site generation and island architecture suit pre-rendered data pages; Chart.js for yield visualizations

backend_stack.value: None; build-time Node.js 20 data pipeline scripts for USDA NASS data ingestion and JSON generation only
backend_stack.rationale: Static website requires no server-side runtime; data transformation runs at build time only

data_platform.value: None; USDA NASS CSV exports processed at build time into static JSON data files stored in /src/data/
data_platform.rationale: No persistent database needed; all data baked into static assets at build time

hosting_target.value: Netlify CDN with global edge distribution, branch deploy previews, and HTTPS enforced
hosting_target.rationale: Netlify provides Git-triggered static deploys, built-in HTTPS, Analytics, and build hooks for scheduled rebuilds

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data collected; Netlify Analytics uses cookieless aggregate fingerprinting with no PII storage
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Public site with no user accounts or form submissions; privacy policy covers analytics only

future_scope.value: Interactive county comparison tool, downloadable GIS shapefiles for pest pressure maps, live USDA NASS API integration for near-real-time statistics in Year 2
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 enhancements requiring JavaScript islands or serverless API routes

constraints.value: Must remain fully static with no server-side runtime on Netlify; build time under 10 minutes; data updates on weekly cadence aligned with USDA NASS Tuesday release schedule
constraints.rationale: No backend infrastructure budget; educators expect data freshness aligned to USDA publication cadence

observability_baseline.value: Netlify Analytics for page views and referrer tracking; GitHub Actions build status badges; Netlify email alerts on build failure
observability_baseline.rationale: Lightweight analytics sufficient for public reference site; no APM or error tracking needed at this scale

execution_preference.value: Continuous delivery on merge to main; weekly scheduled builds triggered via Netlify build hook from GitHub Actions cron on Tuesday mornings
execution_preference.rationale: Automated weekly rebuilds keep statistics current without manual intervention

project.goal: Pre-rendered county crop statistics reference site for extension educators
project.target_users: Extension educators and county agents statewide

frontend.charts: Chart.js 4 for yield trend line charts and acreage bar charts
frontend.search: Pagefind client-side index for crop and county search

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for lint, type-check, data pipeline validation, and Astro build
devops.hosting: Netlify CDN with branch deploy previews
devops.data_pipeline: Node.js 20 script fetching USDA NASS CSV exports, validating with zod, outputting JSON data files

constraints.build_time: Under 10 minutes for full site generation
constraints.data_cadence: Weekly rebuild aligned to USDA NASS Tuesday release schedule

open_questions.pest_map_source: Confirm whether pest pressure ratings come from state extension IPM database or USDA NRCS

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is well-defined for an MVP static reference site. Limiting the first release to the top 10 crops avoids generating hundreds of thin pages and ensures each county/crop combination has meaningful multi-year yield data. The printable fact sheet requirement is straightforward with Astro print CSS.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro content collections with TypeScript schemas are the right fit for typed county and crop data pages. The build-time pipeline should output one JSON file per county containing all crop statistics, enabling Astro to generate pages deterministically. Pagefind can index post-build for client-side crop and county lookup without a search backend.
reasoner_reviews.security: Static sites have a minimal attack surface. The main risks are supply chain compromise via malicious npm packages in the build pipeline and CDN misconfiguration. Enforce CSP headers via netlify.toml, pin npm dependencies with lockfile, and restrict Netlify deploy key scopes to prevent unauthorized deploys.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Weekly scheduled builds are achievable with Netlify build hooks triggered by a GitHub Actions cron job on Tuesday mornings after USDA NASS data drops. Build time under 10 minutes is feasible for a few thousand static pages if USDA data fetch and Astro build are cached between runs.

frontend.app_structure: Astro site with content collections: crops (commodity name, USDA NASS commodity code, description, planting window) and counties (FIPS code, county name, region). Dynamic routes /counties/[county]/crops/[crop] generate one page per county/crop pair with yield table and Chart.js trend chart as an Astro island.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home page with state crop overview. /crops/[slug] listing page by commodity. /counties/[slug] hub page listing all tracked crops. /counties/[county]/crops/[crop] detail page with 10-year yield trend Chart.js chart, harvested vs planted acres bar chart, pest pressure badge, and printable fact sheet button. /search page via Pagefind client-side index.
frontend.performance_notes: All Chart.js islands load only on detail pages via Astro partial hydration with client:visible directive. Pagefind search index loaded lazily on search page interaction. Images compressed with Sharp at build time. Netlify CDN edge cache with 1-week max-age for static assets.
frontend.frontend_risks: Pagefind index size may grow large if all county/crop permutations are indexed at launch; mitigate by indexing only title and summary fields, not full yield tables. Chart.js bundle size adds ~200KB to detail pages; acceptable for extension users on desktop browsers.

security.auth_design: No authentication; site is fully public read-only with no login or session management.
security.abuse_prevention: CSP headers in netlify.toml: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' (Chart.js bundled, no CDN); style-src 'self' (Tailwind purged CSS); frame-ancestors 'none'. No user-submitted forms prevent XSS injection vectors.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Netlify Analytics provides aggregate page view data with no individual user tracking or session storage. No server-side logging infrastructure required.
security.security_risks: Primary risk is build pipeline compromise via malicious npm dependency. Mitigate with npm audit gate in CI blocking high-severity vulnerabilities, pinned lockfile, and restricted Netlify deploy key scopes.

data.entities: county_crop_statistics (county_fips STRING, crop_slug STRING, year INT, yield_per_acre FLOAT, harvested_acres INT, planted_acres INT, state_rank INT), pest_pressure_maps (county_fips STRING, crop_slug STRING, season_year INT, pressure_rating ENUM low/moderate/high/severe, primary_pest_species STRING[]), extension_bulletins (slug STRING, title STRING, crop_slug STRING, published_date DATE, pdf_url STRING, summary STRING), crop_varieties (slug STRING, common_name STRING, nass_commodity_code STRING, typical_planting_window STRING, typical_harvest_window STRING).
data.schema_notes: All entities stored as static JSON files in /src/data/ generated by build pipeline. county_crop_statistics.json keyed by FIPS+crop_slug for O(1) lookup in Astro page generation. pest_pressure_maps.json loaded only on detail pages. Extension bulletins linked per crop slug.
data.data_risks: USDA NASS CSV format changes could break the pipeline parser; mitigate with zod schema validation step that fails the build loudly on unexpected column headers before writing any JSON output.

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub repo main branch triggers Netlify build on merge. Weekly cron build triggered via Netlify build hook from GitHub Actions scheduled workflow (Tuesday 09:15 ET after USDA NASS Tuesday release). Branch deploy previews auto-generated for all PRs. Production served via Netlify CDN with HTTPS enforced globally.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions pipeline: (1) npm ci with cached node_modules, (2) TypeScript type-check and ESLint, (3) node scripts/fetch-usda-data.js with zod schema validation and row count sanity check, (4) astro build, (5) npx pagefind --site dist, (6) Netlify CLI deploy to production. Scheduled weekly workflow calls Netlify build hook via repository secret.
devops.observability_stack: Netlify Analytics dashboard for weekly page view trends, top county and crop pages, and referrer domain distribution. GitHub Actions build status badge on repo README. Netlify email alert on build failure.
devops.devops_risks: USDA NASS CSV download URL changes could silently fail the data pipeline; add HTTP status assertion and CSV column header validation as first pipeline step to catch schema drift before build proceeds.

target_output.thinking_summary: Building a fully static Astro site pre-rendered from USDA NASS county crop data with no server-side runtime, consistent with the static_website project class and Netlify hosting constraint. The key decision is the build-time Node.js data pipeline that transforms USDA CSV exports into typed JSON files consumed by Astro content collections. Pagefind provides client-side search without any backend. Weekly Netlify build hook triggered from GitHub Actions cron keeps statistics current with USDA publication cadence.
target_output.title: County Crop Statistics Reference Site for Agricultural Extension
target_output.executive_summary: A fully static Astro 4 site pre-rendered from USDA NASS county-level crop production data, hosted on Netlify CDN with no server-side infrastructure. Extension educators browse 10-year yield trend charts, pest pressure ratings by season, and printable fact sheets organized by county and commodity. A build-time Node.js pipeline ingests USDA NASS CSV exports weekly, validates column schemas with zod, and regenerates all county/crop pages automatically each Tuesday morning.
target_output.architecture_overview: Static site generation architecture with Astro 4 content collections driving county/crop page routes from build-time JSON data files. A Node.js 20 pipeline script executes at build time: it fetches USDA NASS CSV exports, validates column schemas against zod definitions, and writes normalized JSON files to /src/data/. Astro generates one HTML page per county/crop pair with Chart.js yield trend and acreage charts as client:visible islands hydrated only when visible. Pagefind builds a client-side search index after the Astro build completes. Netlify CDN serves all assets globally with HTTPS enforced and 1-week edge cache headers. No server-side runtime, no database, no authentication layer.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4 with TypeScript content collections, Tailwind CSS v3 (purged), Chart.js 4 via Astro island components with client:visible directive, Pagefind 1.x for client-side search index. Data pipeline: Node.js 20 with csv-parse and zod for USDA NASS CSV ingestion and schema validation. Build tooling: GitHub Actions CI, Netlify CLI for deploys. Analytics: Netlify Analytics cookieless aggregate tracking. Hosting: Netlify CDN with branch previews and HTTPS.
target_output.functional_feature_map: County hub pages listing all tracked commodity crops with latest-year yield summary and current pest pressure badge. Crop/county detail pages with 10-year yield trend Chart.js line chart, harvested vs planted acres bar chart, full multi-year data table, and pest pressure section listing primary pest species by season. Printable fact sheet view activated via browser print dialog with print-optimized CSS layout. Pagefind client-side full-text search returning county hub and crop detail page results. Extension bulletins index linking PDF resources per commodity crop.
target_output.system_components: USDA NASS data pipeline script (Node.js 20, runs at build time): downloads USDA Quick Stats CSV exports, validates headers with zod, merges current season data with historical records, writes county_crop_statistics.json, pest_pressure_maps.json, and extension_bulletins.json to /src/data/. Astro content collection schemas (TypeScript): crops and counties typed definitions. Astro static route generators for /counties/[county]/crops/[crop] detail pages and hub pages. Chart.js Astro island components for yield trend and acreage charts. Pagefind post-build indexer for crop and county search. Netlify CDN edge nodes with HTTPS and cache headers. GitHub Actions CI pipeline with scheduled weekly cron trigger.
target_output.workflows: Build and Data Refresh Workflow: GitHub Actions cron fires Tuesday 09:15 ET. Workflow calls Netlify build hook via stored repository secret. Netlify runner executes Node.js data pipeline (downloads current USDA NASS CSV for all tracked commodities, validates column headers match expected schema, merges new season data into historical JSON files). Astro build generates all county/crop detail pages and hub pages from updated JSON data. Pagefind indexes page titles, summaries, and pest pressure fields. Netlify deploys built assets to CDN. Build failure triggers email alert to site maintainer with step output attached. Extension Educator Research Workflow: Educator visits site and enters a county name or crop commodity into the Pagefind search input. Search index returns matching county hub or crop detail pages ranked by relevance with zero server round-trip. Educator navigates to county/crop detail page, reviews 10-year yield trend chart and pest pressure history, then activates browser print dialog to generate a fact sheet for field use.
target_output.data_model: county_crop_statistics: {county_fips STRING PK+, crop_slug STRING PK+, year INT PK+, yield_per_acre FLOAT, harvested_acres INT, planted_acres INT, state_rank INT, data_source STRING 'USDA_NASS'}. pest_pressure_maps: {county_fips STRING, crop_slug STRING, season_year INT, pressure_rating ENUM low/moderate/high/severe, primary_pest_species STRING[]}. extension_bulletins: {slug STRING, title STRING, crop_slug STRING, published_date DATE, pdf_url STRING, summary STRING}. crop_varieties: {slug STRING, common_name STRING, nass_commodity_code STRING, typical_planting_window STRING, typical_harvest_window STRING}. historical_yield_tables: denormalized 10-year arrays per county/crop pair, embedded in Astro page data at build time for Chart.js rendering. All entities stored as static JSON files in /src/data/ with no runtime database.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Static data access patterns: GET /counties/[fips-slug]/ (county hub, all crops listed), GET /counties/[fips-slug]/crops/[crop-slug]/ (detail page, yield table and chart data embedded as JSON in script tag), GET /search/ (Pagefind client-side index, no server call), GET /crops/[crop-slug]/ (commodity overview across all counties). Build-time external data source: USDA NASS Quick Stats API GET /api/api_GET/ with parameters commodity_desc, state_alpha, year range — HTTP status and column header assertions guard this call before any JSON is written.
target_output.security_and_compliance: CSP headers in netlify.toml: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' (Chart.js bundled at build, no external CDN); style-src 'self' (Tailwind CSS purged and bundled); frame-ancestors 'none'; base-uri 'self'. HTTPS enforced via Netlify with HSTS header. No cookies set; Netlify Analytics uses cookieless aggregate fingerprinting with no PII collection or storage. npm audit gate in GitHub Actions CI blocks builds with high-severity dependency vulnerabilities. Netlify deploy keys scoped to single site; branch protection rule prevents direct pushes to main. USDA NASS API key stored in Netlify environment variable and GitHub Actions secret, never committed to repository.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Netlify site connected to GitHub main branch for continuous deployment on merge. Branch deploy previews auto-generated for all PRs, enabling QA review of data changes and layout updates before merge. Weekly scheduled rebuild: GitHub Actions workflow (schedule: cron '15 9 * * 2') posts to Netlify build hook URL stored as repository secret NETLIFY_BUILD_HOOK_URL. Build pipeline sequence in Netlify runner: (1) npm ci with node_modules cache, (2) node scripts/fetch-usda-data.js executing USDA CSV download, zod schema validation, and JSON write with row count logged to step summary, (3) astro build generating all static pages, (4) npx pagefind --site dist building search index, (5) Netlify CLI deploy to production CDN. Target build duration under 10 minutes. Rollback: Netlify dashboard one-click redeploy to any prior successful deploy. Environment variable USDA_NASS_API_KEY set in Netlify site environment; not exposed in build logs.
target_output.observability: Netlify Analytics dashboard: weekly page views by county and crop detail pages, top referrers (land-grant university extension domains, state department of agriculture sites), geographic visitor distribution. GitHub Actions: build status badge on repository README showing last build result. Data pipeline step summary logged to GitHub Actions for each weekly run: rows parsed per commodity, counties covered count, validation errors (should be 0, failures halt build). Netlify email notification on build failure delivered to site maintainer within 5 minutes of failure.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Netlify Starter free tier covers static hosting for this scale (estimated 3,000-5,000 static pages, low-to-moderate traffic). USDA NASS Quick Stats API key free for public data access. GitHub Actions free tier covers weekly scheduled builds and CI runs. Total estimated cost $0/month on free tiers, scaling to Netlify Pro ($19/month) only if bandwidth exceeds 100GB. Chart.js bundled at build eliminates third-party CDN dependency costs. Netlify CDN automatically absorbs traffic spikes during spring planting season when extension educator usage peaks.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): USDA NASS data pipeline for top 10 commodity crops (corn, soybeans, wheat, cotton, sorghum, oats, barley, sunflowers, alfalfa, dry beans) across all state counties. Astro county hub and crop detail pages with 10-year yield data tables. Chart.js yield trend line chart per detail page. Netlify deploy with HTTPS and weekly scheduled rebuild. Phase 2 (2 weeks): Pagefind client-side search index across all county and crop pages. Pest pressure ratings integrated from state extension IPM database CSV export. Print-optimized CSS layout for browser print fact sheet export. Phase 3 (2 weeks): Extension bulletins index per commodity crop with PDF download links. Full crop coverage expanded beyond top 10 commodities. County comparison page enabling side-by-side yield view for two selected counties.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in Astro content collection schemas and data pipeline scripts. Zod schema validation on all USDA NASS CSV column headers before writing any JSON output, failing the build loudly on unexpected column names. ESLint with Astro plugin runs in CI on every PR. Pagefind re-indexed on every build to reflect current data structure. USDA NASS API key accessed only via environment variable, never hardcoded. County FIPS code used as canonical identifier throughout, not county name string, to handle naming ambiguities. Chart.js imported only inside Astro island components marked client:visible, never in layout or page shell.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: USDA NASS CSV schema changes break the data pipeline parser, silently producing malformed JSON. Mitigation: zod schema validation asserts expected column headers as first pipeline step; build halts and maintainer receives email alert before any JSON is written. Risk: Pagefind index grows prohibitively large as crop/county permutations increase toward full commodity coverage. Mitigation: index only title, summary, and pest rating fields, not full yield tables. Tradeoff: fully static pre-rendered approach vs live USDA NASS API queries. Static chosen for zero-cost hosting, offline educator access, and sub-50ms page loads; accepted tradeoff is up to 1-week data staleness between USDA Tuesday releases.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Pest pressure data source confirmed as state extension IPM database CSV export, not USDA NRCS. USDA NASS Quick Stats API selected over direct FTP CSV downloads for more stable URL structure and programmatic filtering by commodity and state. Netlify Analytics chosen over Google Analytics to avoid GDPR cookie consent banner on a public government-adjacent resource with no existing consent infrastructure.

metadata.notes: Static Astro site pre-rendered from USDA NASS county crop data; build-time data pipeline; Netlify CDN; no server-side runtime.

profile.domain: Agricultural GIS and Land Data Management

project_goal.value: CLI tool that validates and transforms agricultural field boundary shapefiles (GeoJSON and ESRI Shapefile formats) for submission to the USDA FSA Common Land Unit registry, checking topology errors, reprojecting coordinates, and generating compliance-ready output archives
project_goal.rationale: Farm data specialists need an automated pipeline to prepare CLU submission packages without manual GIS desktop steps

target_users.value: Farm data specialists and precision agriculture consultants preparing USDA FSA CLU boundary submissions for enrolled farm operations
target_users.rationale: Primary users are GIS-literate agricultural data managers running batch validation workflows

project_class.rationale: Batch file processing workflow with no UI requirement; runs on local workstation or CI runner against directories of shapefiles

capabilities.rationale: Geospatial processing with GDAL/OGR, validation reporting, and FSA submission package generation; no frontend or auth required

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-step geospatial processing pipeline with topology validation, CRS reprojection, and structured validation reporting

risk_level.rationale: Local tool processing farm boundary files with no external data exposure or financial transactions

data_sensitivity.rationale: Field boundary shapefiles are proprietary farm spatial data; processed locally, never transmitted externally by the tool

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs entirely on local workstation or CI runner; no network exposure except optional FSA submission upload step

access_model.value: Local execution only; no authentication required; file system permissions govern input/output directory access
access_model.rationale: CLI tool operating on local files with no multi-user access control requirements

feature_scope.value: Validate GeoJSON and Shapefile inputs for topology errors (self-intersections, slivers, unclosed rings), reproject to FSA-required CRS (EPSG:4326), generate per-field error code report, pack validated boundaries into submission-ready ZIP archive with metadata manifest
feature_scope.rationale: Full validation and packaging workflow required for FSA CLU submission compliance

mvp_scope.value: GeoJSON input validation with topology checks and EPSG:4326 reprojection; per-field validation report as JSON and CSV; ZIP archive packaging with manifest; Shapefile input support deferred to Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: GeoJSON is the more common input format; Shapefile support added once core pipeline proven

security_baseline.value: Input file path validation to prevent directory traversal; no network calls except optional FSA SFTP upload; dependency pinning with pip-audit in CI
security_baseline.rationale: Local CLI with no network exposure; primary risk is malformed input files and supply chain vulnerabilities

frontend_stack.value: None; CLI output to stdout and JSON/CSV report files only
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI tool with no browser UI or desktop GUI; structured text output for human and machine consumption

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with GDAL/OGR bindings for geospatial operations, Click for CLI interface, Shapely for topology validation
backend_stack.rationale: GDAL/OGR is the standard geospatial processing library with comprehensive CRS support; Click provides clean command/flag interface

data_platform.value: None persistent; input files read from local filesystem, output written to specified output directory; no database
data_platform.rationale: Stateless file-transform CLI requires no persistent storage

hosting_target.value: Local workstation installation via pip or pipx; optional CI runner execution in GitHub Actions
hosting_target.rationale: Distributed as a PyPI package for local install; supports batch execution in CI pipelines

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained by the tool; all output written to user-specified directories; tool logs written to stdout only
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Local processing tool with no cloud storage or telemetry

future_scope.value: ESRI Shapefile and KML input support, direct USDA FSA SFTP submission integration, multi-farm batch processing with summary dashboard HTML report
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 and 3 roadmap expanding input formats and submission automation

constraints.value: Must run on Python 3.12 on Windows 10/11 and macOS 14+; GDAL installed as system dependency or via conda; no cloud dependencies in core validation path
constraints.rationale: Farm data specialists primarily use Windows workstations; GDAL is standard in agricultural GIS environments

observability_baseline.value: Structured JSON validation report per run written to output directory; verbose flag enables per-field processing logs to stdout; exit codes signal success or validation failures for CI integration
observability_baseline.rationale: CLI tools need machine-readable exit codes and structured reports for CI integration and audit trails

execution_preference.value: Single-command invocation with sensible defaults; installable via pip install fsa-clv-validator; CI-ready with non-zero exit on validation errors
execution_preference.rationale: Farm data specialists expect simple command invocation; CI integration requires exit code conventions

project.goal: CLI tool for FSA CLU shapefile validation and submission packaging
project.target_users: Farm data specialists and ag consultants on Windows and macOS

backend.geospatial: GDAL/OGR with pyogrio, Shapely 2.x for topology
backend.cli: Click 8.x with subcommands and flag parsing
backend.reporting: JSON and CSV validation report output

data.output_formats: JSON validation report, CSV error table, submission-ready ZIP archive with manifest.json

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for pytest, pip-audit, and build wheel
devops.distribution: PyPI package via Twine; pipx install supported

constraints.gdal: Requires GDAL 3.8+ as system or conda dependency

open_questions.windows_gdal: Confirm GDAL installation approach for Windows users: conda vs OSGeo4W vs bundled wheels

reasoner_reviews.product: The CLI scope is well-constrained for an initial release. Focusing on GeoJSON input first and deferring Shapefile to Phase 2 is the right call given GeoJSON is increasingly the standard exchange format for ag platforms. The per-field error code output is critical for specialists who need to communicate specific issues back to farmers.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Click subcommands (validate, transform, package) allow clean separation of concerns and composability in CI pipelines. GDAL/OGR handles both CRS reprojection and format conversion natively, reducing the need for additional geospatial dependencies. Shapely 2.x provides significantly faster topology operations than Shapely 1.x thanks to the GEOS 3.11 backend.
reasoner_reviews.security: The primary risk for a local file-processing tool is directory traversal via crafted input paths. Click's path validation and explicit input/output directory arguments mitigate this. pip-audit in CI catches vulnerable transitive dependencies in the GDAL Python bindings.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GDAL installation on Windows is the biggest friction point for end users. The README should document the conda install path as the recommended approach, with OSGeo4W as an alternative. The CI matrix should test on both ubuntu-latest and windows-latest runners to catch platform-specific issues.

backend.service_design: Click CLI with three subcommands: 'validate' (run topology and schema checks, emit JSON report), 'transform' (reproject to EPSG:4326, normalize attribute names to FSA CLU spec), 'package' (assemble validated fields into submission ZIP with manifest.json). Each subcommand is an independent Click command registered on the main group, enabling composable pipeline use.
backend.api_patterns: CLI flags: --input-dir PATH (required), --output-dir PATH (default ./output), --crs EPSG_CODE (default 4326), --format [geojson|shapefile] (default geojson), --verbose (enable per-field processing logs), --strict (fail on warnings in addition to errors). Exit codes: 0=success, 1=validation errors found, 2=tool error (unreadable input, GDAL failure).
backend.business_modules: topology_validator.py: wraps Shapely 2.x is_valid(), explain_validity(), and custom sliver detection (area/perimeter ratio threshold). crs_transformer.py: GDAL/OGR CRS detection and reprojection pipeline. report_generator.py: builds ValidationReport dataclass with per-field FieldResult entries, serializes to JSON and CSV. packager.py: assembles output directory into ZIP archive with auto-generated manifest.json listing field count, CRS, validation summary, and submission timestamp.
backend.failure_handling: GDAL errors caught and surfaced as structured FieldResult entries with error_code FSA_GDAL_ERR rather than unhandled exceptions. Malformed GeoJSON files logged with file path and parse error; processing continues to next file (soft failure). Unreadable input directory raises ToolError with exit code 2. --strict flag converts warnings to errors for stricter CI gating.

security.auth_design: No authentication; local filesystem execution only.
security.abuse_prevention: Input path validation using Click's Path(exists=True, file_okay=False, dir_okay=True) type to prevent non-existent or file-path injection. Output directory created only under user-specified --output-dir to prevent writes outside intended scope.
security.security_risks: Supply chain risk from GDAL Python bindings transitive dependencies. pip-audit CI gate checks for known CVEs. Pinned dependency versions in requirements.txt and pyproject.toml.

data.entities: field_boundaries (fid STRING, geometry GeoJSON polygon, source_crs STRING, area_ha FLOAT, perimeter_m FLOAT, attribute_map DICT), topology_errors (fid STRING, error_code ENUM self_intersection/sliver/unclosed_ring/degenerate, error_detail STRING, severity ENUM error/warning), clu_records (fid STRING, reprojected_geometry GeoJSON EPSG:4326, normalized_attributes DICT, validation_status ENUM passed/failed/warning), submission_reports (run_timestamp DATETIME, input_dir STRING, field_count INT, passed_count INT, failed_count INT, warning_count INT, output_zip_path STRING).
data.schema_notes: All entities exist only in memory during a CLI run; serialized to JSON report and CSV on completion. No persistent database. ValidationReport dataclass is the central data object passed between pipeline stages.
data.data_risks: Input GeoJSON files with non-standard geometry types (MultiPolygon with >1 ring part) require explicit handling in topology validator to avoid false-positive self-intersection errors on valid complex boundaries.

devops.deployment_topology: PyPI package 'fsa-clu-validator' installable via pip or pipx. CI matrix: ubuntu-latest and windows-latest GitHub Actions runners with Python 3.12 and GDAL 3.8 via conda-ci action. PyPI release triggered on version tag push via Twine.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions pipeline: (1) conda setup with GDAL 3.8, (2) pip install -e '.[dev]', (3) pip-audit for CVE check, (4) pytest with coverage gate 85%, (5) build wheel via python -m build, (6) on tag: twine upload to PyPI with stored PYPI_TOKEN secret.
devops.devops_risks: Windows GitHub Actions runner GDAL conda installation adds 3-5 minutes to CI time; cache the conda environment between runs using actions/cache keyed on environment.yml hash.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a Python CLI tool with Click subcommands for FSA CLU shapefile validation. The project_class is cli_tool so there is no FrontendAgent subplan. GDAL/OGR handles CRS reprojection and Shapely handles topology validation. Key design decision: three composable subcommands (validate, transform, package) with structured exit codes for CI integration. No database or network required in the core validation path.
target_output.title: FSA CLU Field Boundary Validator CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 CLI tool using Click subcommands that validates agricultural field boundary files (GeoJSON) for USDA FSA Common Land Unit registry submission. It checks topology errors via Shapely, reprojects coordinates to EPSG:4326 via GDAL/OGR, generates per-field JSON and CSV validation reports with FSA-specific error codes, and packages validated boundaries into submission-ready ZIP archives with auto-generated manifests. Distributable via pip and usable in CI pipelines with structured exit codes.
target_output.architecture_overview: Stateless file-processing CLI with three Click subcommands: validate, transform, and package. The validate subcommand reads GeoJSON features from an input directory via pyogrio, runs each boundary through the topology_validator module (Shapely 2.x is_valid check, sliver detection by area/perimeter ratio, unclosed ring detection), and emits a ValidationReport with per-field FieldResult entries. The transform subcommand applies GDAL/OGR CRS reprojection to EPSG:4326 and normalizes attribute names to FSA CLU schema. The package subcommand assembles a submission-ready ZIP archive with manifest.json summarizing field count, validation status, and submission timestamp. Pipeline stages are composable: each subcommand reads validated output from the previous stage's output directory, enabling full pipeline execution as three sequential CLI invocations or a single pipeline script.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12. CLI framework: Click 8.x with subcommands, path validation types, and exit code conventions. Geospatial: GDAL 3.8 with pyogrio for fast GeoJSON/Shapefile I/O, Shapely 2.x (GEOS 3.11 backend) for topology validation. Reporting: built-in json and csv modules. Distribution: pyproject.toml with hatchling build backend, PyPI distribution via Twine. Testing: pytest 8 with pytest-cov. CI: GitHub Actions matrix on ubuntu-latest and windows-latest with conda GDAL setup.
target_output.functional_feature_map: validate subcommand: reads all .geojson files in input directory, runs per-feature topology checks (self-intersection via Shapely is_valid, sliver detection area/perimeter ratio, unclosed ring via geometry validity), outputs JSON report (ValidationReport with FieldResult per feature) and CSV error table with columns fid, error_code, error_detail, severity. transform subcommand: reads GeoJSON input, detects source CRS, reprojects all features to EPSG:4326 via GDAL OGR CoordinateTransformation, normalizes CLU attribute names (tract_number, farm_number, field_number, calculated_area_ha), writes transformed GeoJSON to output directory. package subcommand: assembles output directory contents into ZIP archive, generates manifest.json with field_count, passed_count, failed_count, crs, submission_timestamp, tool_version fields.
target_output.system_components: cli.py: Click group with validate, transform, package subcommands and shared --output-dir, --verbose options. topology_validator.py: FieldValidator class wrapping Shapely is_valid(), explain_validity(), and SliverDetector (configurable area/perimeter threshold defaulting to 0.001). crs_transformer.py: CRSTransformer class using GDAL OGR SpatialReference and CoordinateTransformation for source CRS detection and reprojection to target EPSG code. report_generator.py: ValidationReport and FieldResult dataclasses, serialize_json() and serialize_csv() methods. packager.py: ZipPackager assembling output directory into submission ZIP and generating manifest.json. models.py: shared dataclasses for FieldBoundary, TopologyError, CLURecord, SubmissionManifest.
target_output.workflows: Validation Workflow: Farm data specialist runs 'fsa-clu-validator validate --input-dir ./farm_boundaries --output-dir ./validation_output'. CLI iterates over .geojson files, loads each via pyogrio, runs Shapely topology checks per feature, collects FieldResult objects. On completion writes validation_report.json (full structured report) and validation_errors.csv (tabular error summary) to output directory. Exits 0 if no errors, 1 if any error-severity findings, enabling CI gating. Submission Packaging Workflow: After validation passes, specialist runs 'fsa-clu-validator transform --input-dir ./validation_output --output-dir ./transformed' then 'fsa-clu-validator package --input-dir ./transformed --output-dir ./submission'. Package subcommand creates submission_YYYY-MM-DD.zip containing transformed GeoJSON files and manifest.json. Specialist uploads ZIP to USDA FSA eSubmit portal.
target_output.data_model: field_boundaries: in-memory FieldBoundary dataclass {fid: str, geometry: shapely.Polygon, source_crs: str, area_ha: float, perimeter_m: float, raw_attributes: dict}. topology_errors: TopologyError dataclass {fid: str, error_code: Enum[self_intersection, sliver, unclosed_ring, degenerate], error_detail: str, severity: Enum[error, warning]}. clu_records: CLURecord dataclass {fid: str, reprojected_geom: dict GeoJSON, tract_number: str, farm_number: str, field_number: str, calculated_area_ha: float, validation_status: Enum[passed, failed, warning]}. submission_manifests: SubmissionManifest dataclass {run_timestamp: datetime, input_dir: str, field_count: int, passed_count: int, failed_count: int, warning_count: int, output_zip_path: str, tool_version: str}. ValidationReport: {run_timestamp: datetime, summary: SubmissionManifest, field_results: list[FieldResult]}.
target_output.api_design: CLI command interface: 'fsa-clu-validator validate --input-dir PATH [--output-dir PATH] [--verbose] [--strict]' — validates all GeoJSON files, writes JSON+CSV report, exits 0/1/2. 'fsa-clu-validator transform --input-dir PATH [--output-dir PATH] [--target-crs EPSG:4326]' — reprojects and normalizes attributes, writes transformed GeoJSON files. 'fsa-clu-validator package --input-dir PATH [--output-dir PATH] [--submission-name STRING]' — creates submission ZIP with manifest. 'fsa-clu-validator --version' — prints package version. Programmatic API: from fsa_clu_validator import FieldValidator, CRSTransformer, ZipPackager for embedding in larger data pipelines.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Input directory validation via Click Path(exists=True, file_okay=False, dir_okay=True, resolve_path=True) preventing relative path traversal. Output written only under explicitly specified --output-dir, never to system directories. USDA FSA CLU submission standards compliance: EPSG:4326 CRS enforcement, required attribute fields (tract_number, farm_number, field_number) checked in transform stage. pip-audit gate in GitHub Actions CI blocks builds with high-severity CVEs in GDAL bindings or Shapely transitive dependencies. Dependency versions pinned in pyproject.toml with upper bounds to prevent surprise breakage on GDAL major version releases.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Distribution: PyPI package 'fsa-clu-validator' built with hatchling via 'python -m build', published via Twine on tag push from GitHub Actions. Install: 'pip install fsa-clu-validator' (requires GDAL 3.8+ pre-installed) or 'conda install -c conda-forge fsa-clu-validator gdal'. GitHub Actions CI matrix: ubuntu-latest (GDAL via apt gdal-bin) and windows-latest (GDAL via conda-forge). CI steps: (1) conda setup with environment.yml pinning GDAL 3.8, Python 3.12, (2) pip install -e '.[dev]', (3) pip-audit, (4) pytest -v --cov=fsa_clu_validator --cov-report=xml with 85% coverage gate, (5) python -m build wheel and sdist. Release workflow triggered on 'v*' tag: builds wheel and publishes to PyPI using stored PYPI_TOKEN secret. README documents conda install as recommended path for Windows users.
target_output.observability: Exit codes as primary CI signal: 0=all fields passed, 1=validation errors found (review report), 2=tool error (unreadable input, GDAL failure). Structured validation_report.json written to output directory on every run, containing per-field results with error codes for audit trail. --verbose flag streams per-field processing log lines to stdout (field ID, topology check results, CRS source). CI step summary integration: GitHub Actions step can read exit code and attach validation_report.json as artifact. Tool version and GDAL version logged in ValidationReport.tool_version field for reproducibility tracking.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero ongoing infrastructure cost; all processing runs locally or in CI runners. GitHub Actions free tier covers CI runs. PyPI hosting free for open-source package. GDAL processing speed: Shapely 2.x GEOS 3.11 handles 10,000 field boundaries in under 30 seconds on a modern workstation. For batch processing hundreds of farms, a simple Python multiprocessing pool over input directories can parallelize GDAL reprojection across CPU cores.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): GeoJSON input validation with Shapely topology checks (self-intersection, sliver, unclosed ring), EPSG:4326 reprojection via GDAL, JSON and CSV validation report, submission ZIP packaging with manifest.json, pip-installable package with pytest coverage gate 85%. Phase 2 (2 weeks): ESRI Shapefile input support via pyogrio Shapefile reader, KML input via GDAL KML driver, --strict mode for CI hard-fail on warnings. Phase 3 (2 weeks): Direct USDA FSA SFTP submission integration via paramiko, HTML summary report output for non-technical users, multi-farm batch processing mode with per-farm sub-reports.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 type hints throughout with mypy strict mode in CI. Dataclasses preferred over plain dicts for all internal data models (FieldBoundary, TopologyError, CLURecord). Click Path type validation used for all filesystem arguments to catch input errors early. GDAL errors caught at the OGR layer and surfaced as structured FieldResult entries rather than unhandled exceptions. pytest fixtures provide sample GeoJSON files with known topology errors for deterministic unit testing. All topology thresholds (sliver ratio, minimum area) configurable via Click options with documented FSA-recommended defaults.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GDAL installation friction on Windows workstations breaks adoption by farm data specialists unfamiliar with conda. Mitigation: document conda-forge install path prominently in README; provide OSGeo4W alternative; consider bundled GDAL wheels for Windows in a future release. Risk: MultiPolygon inputs with legitimate complex boundaries (fields with internal exclusion zones) trigger false-positive self-intersection errors. Mitigation: detect MultiPolygon geometry type and apply per-ring validation rather than whole-geometry is_valid check. Tradeoff: stateless CLI design means no incremental processing across runs; all fields in input directory revalidated each run. Acceptable for the current batch sizes (hundreds not thousands of fields per farm operation).
target_output.open_questions_resolved: GDAL installation approach for Windows confirmed as conda-forge recommended path, with OSGeo4W documented as alternative. CRS target confirmed as EPSG:4326 per USDA FSA CLU submission specification. Sliver detection threshold (area/perimeter ratio 0.001) calibrated against FSA minimum field size requirements of 0.1 acres.

metadata.notes: Python CLI with Click subcommands for FSA CLU shapefile topology validation and submission packaging; GDAL/OGR and Shapely 2.x; no frontend specialist.

project_goal.value: High-throughput REST API serving real-time commodity spot and futures price data to agricultural trading desks and farm management software clients, aggregating feeds from multiple exchanges and normalizing prices into a unified schema with sub-second latency
project_goal.rationale: Agricultural trading desks require normalized cross-exchange price data with consistent schema and predictable low latency

target_users.value: Agricultural trading desks, farm management software vendors, and commodity analytics platforms consuming market data via partner API agreements
target_users.rationale: B2B partner clients with contractual SLA requirements for data freshness and availability

project_class.rationale: Pure backend API with no frontend; serves partner clients exclusively via REST and WebSocket endpoints

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, public_api, realtime, analytics
capabilities.rationale: High-throughput price ingestion, partner authentication, REST and WebSocket delivery, ClickHouse analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-exchange feed aggregation at tick frequency with sub-second Redis hot-cache delivery to hundreds of concurrent partner clients

risk_level.rationale: Financial data with contractual accuracy and uptime SLAs; exchange license violation risk for unauthorized redistribution

data_sensitivity.rationale: Commodity price data subject to exchange licensing restrictions and CFTC regulations on redistribution

external_exposure.rationale: API accessible only to credentialed partner organizations with signed data redistribution agreements

access_model.value: Rotating API keys per partner organization with per-key rate limits; mTLS required for exchange feed adapter connections
access_model.rationale: Partner API key rotation enforces license accountability; mTLS secures inbound exchange feeds

feature_scope.value: Multi-exchange feed adapters (CME, ICE), canonical price normalization, REST spot and futures price endpoints, WebSocket price stream subscriptions, API key management with rate limiting, ClickHouse historical price analytics, exchange license compliance enforcement per partner
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for agricultural commodity data product

mvp_scope.value: CME feed adapter, REST spot and front-month futures endpoints for corn, soybeans, wheat, cotton; Redis hot-cache for sub-second responses; API key auth with rate limiting; ClickHouse tick storage; ICE and additional commodities in Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: CME covers majority of agricultural futures volume; validates architecture before adding ICE adapter complexity

security_baseline.value: mTLS for exchange feed connections, API key authentication with HMAC-SHA256 signature validation, TLS 1.3 for all partner API traffic, rate limiting enforced in Redis, API gateway WAF
security_baseline.rationale: Financial data API requires strong authentication and transport security; mTLS prevents feed spoofing

frontend_stack.value: None; API service with no browser UI
frontend_stack.rationale: Pure backend API service; partner clients build their own UI consuming this API

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin HTTP framework; goroutine-per-feed-adapter model for concurrent exchange ingestion
backend_stack.rationale: Go's concurrency model and low GC pause times are ideal for high-throughput tick ingestion and sub-second response latency requirements

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for tick-level historical price storage and analytics queries; Redis 7 for hot-cache of latest spot and front-month futures prices per commodity
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage handles billions of price ticks with fast range aggregation; Redis provides sub-millisecond hot-cache reads

hosting_target.value: AWS EKS with Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler; ClickHouse on dedicated EC2 instances; Redis via ElastiCache
hosting_target.rationale: EKS enables rapid horizontal scaling for feed adapter and API handler pods independently; dedicated ClickHouse nodes prevent noisy-neighbor I/O contention

privacy_retention_policy.value: Price tick data retained 5 years in ClickHouse per exchange licensing agreements; API access logs retained 1 year for CFTC audit purposes; no personal data collected beyond partner organization contact metadata
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Exchange license terms and CFTC record-keeping requirements dictate minimum and maximum retention periods

future_scope.value: ICE feed adapter, options chain data, WebSocket push subscriptions for real-time price streams, partner self-service key management portal, historical bulk export API
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 and 3 roadmap expanding exchange coverage and delivery mechanisms

constraints.value: P99 API response latency under 50ms for cached price reads; 99.9% monthly uptime SLA; exchange license restricts redistribution to credentialed partners only; no price data persisted outside AWS us-east-1 region
constraints.rationale: SLA commitments to partner contracts; exchange license data sovereignty requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with distributed tracing across feed adapter and API handler services; custom metrics for tick ingestion rate, cache hit ratio, and per-partner rate limit consumption; PagerDuty escalation
observability_baseline.rationale: Financial data API requires real-time visibility into feed health and SLA compliance metrics

execution_preference.value: Continuous deployment with canary releases on EKS; blue-green switch for ClickHouse schema migrations
execution_preference.rationale: High-availability requirement prevents full rolling restarts during peak trading hours

compliance_context.value: CFTC commodity data regulations for price data redistribution; exchange licensing agreements (CME Market Data Policy, ICE Data Services terms); contractual partner data redistribution restrictions
compliance_context.rationale: Financial commodity data redistribution requires CFTC compliance and strict adherence to exchange licensing terms

project.goal: Sub-second commodity price API for agricultural trading and farm management clients
project.target_users: B2B partner organizations with signed data agreements

backend.feed_ingestion: CME FIX/FAST protocol adapter, goroutine pool per commodity group
backend.caching: Redis HSET per commodity contract for hot-cache reads

security.auth: API key with HMAC-SHA256 request signature
security.transport: TLS 1.3 for partner API; mTLS for exchange feed connections
security.rate_limiting: Redis sliding window per API key per minute

data.tick_store: ClickHouse with price_ticks table, MergeTree engine, partition by toYYYYMM(tick_time)
data.hot_cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache with 10s TTL for spot and front-month futures

devops.environments: dev, staging, production

reasoner_reviews.product: The Round 1 plan delivered solid CME feed adapter and core REST endpoints. The main gap identified in audit was insufficient detail on exchange license compliance enforcement per partner client. The rate limiting implementation also needed explicit per-commodity-group quota support beyond a flat per-key limit.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The Go Gin service design is appropriate for the throughput target. The Round 1 plan lacked explicit detail on the ClickHouse ReplacingMergeTree deduplication strategy for handling CME feed retransmissions. The feed adapter goroutine pool sizing for CME FAST protocol also needed documentation. Address these in Round 2.
reasoner_reviews.security: Round 1 plan specified mTLS for exchange feeds but did not detail the certificate rotation process for exchange-issued client certificates. Partner API key compromise scenario also lacked a documented revocation and re-issuance workflow. Both gaps are blocking issues for the security rubric.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: P99 50ms SLA requires Redis hot-cache hit rate above 99% for spot price reads. The Round 1 plan did not specify cache warm-up strategy on pod startup to prevent cold-start latency spikes when new feed adapter pods come online.

backend.service_design: Two Kubernetes deployments: feed-adapter (CME FIX/FAST goroutine pool, normalizes ticks to CanonicalPriceTick struct, writes to ClickHouse and Redis) and api-handler (Gin server, reads Redis hot-cache for price endpoints, ClickHouse for historical queries, enforces API key auth and rate limits via middleware). Independent HPA scaling for each deployment.
backend.api_patterns: REST: GET /v1/prices/spot/{commodity} (Redis cache, P99 <15ms), GET /v1/prices/futures/{commodity}/{expiry} (Redis cache), GET /v1/prices/history/{commodity}?from=&to=&resolution= (ClickHouse, P99 <500ms). Rate limiting middleware checks Redis sliding window counter before routing. API key validation middleware verifies HMAC-SHA256 request signature.
backend.failure_handling: CME feed reconnect with exponential backoff (initial 1s, max 60s, jitter). Redis write failures logged and retried up to 3 times; ClickHouse write failures buffered in in-memory ring buffer (1M tick capacity) and flushed on reconnect. API gateway returns 503 with Retry-After header when feed adapter is disconnected beyond 30s stale threshold.
backend.scaling_notes: Feed adapter HPA: 2-10 pods on CPU 70% target (commodity group partitioning ensures each pod handles a subset of CME symbols). API handler HPA: 3-30 pods on RPS target 5000/pod. Redis ElastiCache cluster mode with 3 shards handles concurrent hot-cache writes from feed adapters and reads from API handlers.

security.auth_design: API key authentication: partner sends X-API-Key and X-Signature headers. API handler middleware validates key existence in Redis key registry, verifies HMAC-SHA256(secret, method+path+timestamp) signature, and checks timestamp within 30s to prevent replay attacks. mTLS client certificates for CME feed connections rotated 90 days via AWS ACM Private CA.
security.authorization_model: Per-partner API key metadata in Redis: {commodities_allowed: [CORN, SOYBEANS], rate_limit_rpm: 10000, license_tier: standard/premium, expiry_date}. Commodity access enforced per-request against partner's allowed commodities list, rejecting unauthorized commodity queries with 403 and compliance log entry.
security.secrets_management: Exchange API credentials and CME certificate private keys in AWS Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation. Partner API key secrets stored encrypted in Redis with AES-256-GCM. Key revocation: DELETE /internal/admin/keys/{key_id} endpoint marks key as revoked in Redis registry; revocation effective within 1s propagation.
security.security_risks: Exchange license violation risk if partner key is compromised and used for unauthorized redistribution. Mitigate with per-key rate limits, commodity-level access control enforcement at API layer, and automated suspicious-usage alerts (>2x average RPS for 5 minutes triggers PagerDuty).

data.entities: commodity_contracts (contract_id STRING, exchange ENUM cme/ice, commodity ENUM corn/soybeans/wheat/cotton/sorghum, expiry_date DATE, tick_size FLOAT, contract_size FLOAT), spot_price_ticks (contract_id STRING, exchange STRING, bid FLOAT, ask FLOAT, last FLOAT, volume INT, tick_time DATETIME, ingestion_time DATETIME), futures_price_snapshots (contract_id STRING, expiry_date DATE, settlement_price FLOAT, open_interest INT, snapshot_time DATETIME), api_clients (key_id STRING, org_name STRING, commodities_allowed STRING[], rate_limit_rpm INT, license_tier STRING, created_at DATETIME, expires_at DATETIME), rate_limit_quotas (key_id STRING, window_start DATETIME, request_count INT).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse price_ticks table: MergeTree engine partitioned by toYYYYMM(tick_time), ordered by (contract_id, tick_time). Deduplication via ReplacingMergeTree on (contract_id, tick_time, exchange) handles CME retransmissions. Redis hot-cache: HSET commodity:{contract_id} with fields bid, ask, last, volume, updated_at, TTL 10s. Redis rate limit counters: sorted set per key_id with sliding window of last 60s request timestamps.
data.retention_and_deletion: ClickHouse price_ticks: 5-year retention via TTL expression on tick_time. Annual cold tier migration to S3 Parquet via ClickHouse S3 table function for ticks older than 2 years. API access logs in CloudWatch: 1-year retention per CFTC audit requirement.

devops.deployment_topology: AWS EKS us-east-1 with two node groups: compute-optimized for feed-adapter pods (C6i.2xlarge, NUMA-pinned for low-latency FAST protocol parsing), memory-optimized for api-handler pods (R6i.xlarge for Redis connection pool). ClickHouse on dedicated i4i.4xlarge EC2 instances (NVMe local storage) in same VPC. ElastiCache Redis cluster mode (r7g.xlarge, 3 shards) in private subnet.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: (1) go vet and golangci-lint, (2) go test with race detector, (3) docker build and push to ECR, (4) Helm upgrade --install with canary weight 10% on production, (5) automated P99 latency check via Datadog SLO API after canary, (6) promote to 100% or rollback based on SLO check result.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM: distributed traces across feed-adapter and api-handler services with span tags for commodity, exchange, partner_key_id. Custom Datadog metrics: cmc.tick_ingestion_rate (ticks/sec per exchange), cmc.cache_hit_ratio (should be >99%), cmc.api_rps_per_partner (per key_id), cmc.feed_lag_ms (ingestion_time - tick_time). PagerDuty: feed lag >500ms, cache hit ratio <98%, P99 latency >50ms, any feed adapter disconnection.
devops.rollback_strategy: Canary deployment with automatic rollback if P99 latency SLO breached within 5 minutes of canary promotion. Helm release history retained for 5 revisions. ClickHouse schema migrations via blue-green: new schema deployed alongside existing, feed adapter writes to both during migration window, old table dropped after validation.

ISS-001.title: Exchange certificate rotation process undocumented
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan specified mTLS for CME feed connections but did not describe how CME-issued client certificates are rotated before expiry or how feed adapters handle certificate renewal without service interruption.

history.note: Identified by security reviewer in Round 1 audit

ISS-002.title: ClickHouse deduplication strategy not specified for CME retransmissions
ISS-002.detail: CME FIX/FAST protocol retransmits ticks during recovery windows. Round 1 plan did not specify how duplicate ticks are prevented from inflating historical price data in ClickHouse.

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner in Round 1 review

ISS-003.title: Redis cache warm-up strategy missing for pod startup
ISS-003.detail: New feed-adapter pods starting cold will have empty Redis hot-cache entries for their assigned commodity group, causing P99 latency spikes on first requests until cache populates from incoming ticks.

history.note: Identified by constraints reviewer

Specify ClickHouse ReplacingMergeTree deduplication key for CME retransmission handling

Security and data quality sections have blocking gaps that prevent passing: mTLS certificate rotation process is undocumented, and ClickHouse deduplication strategy for CME retransmissions is absent.
strengths: Go goroutine-per-commodity-group model is well-suited for concurrent exchange feed ingestion
Redis hot-cache design with 10s TTL correctly targets sub-50ms P99 for spot price reads
Canary deployment with automated SLO check is appropriate for financial API
concerns: CME client certificate renewal process during active feed connections not described
ClickHouse MergeTree without deduplication will accumulate duplicate ticks during CME recovery windows

No deduplication mechanism specified for CME FIX/FAST retransmission ticks in ClickHouse

Switch ClickHouse price_ticks to ReplacingMergeTree keyed on (contract_id, tick_time, exchange) to deduplicate retransmitted ticks during OPTIMIZE TABLE runs
Add startup probe delay and cache backfill goroutine that loads last 100 ticks per commodity from ClickHouse into Redis on pod init

previous_plan.title: Agricultural Commodity Price API v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Go 1.22 REST API serving normalized CME commodity price data to partner clients via Redis hot-cache with sub-50ms P99. Feed adapters ingest CME FIX/FAST ticks, normalize to CanonicalPriceTick, write to ClickHouse and Redis. Partner API key auth with HMAC-SHA256 and rate limiting.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Go Gin API handler pods (EKS) reading Redis ElastiCache hot-cache for price endpoints; feed adapter pods ingesting CME FAST ticks via goroutine pools; ClickHouse on dedicated EC2 for tick history. Redis sliding window rate limiting per partner key.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: mTLS for CME feed connections, API key HMAC-SHA256 auth, TLS 1.3 for partner traffic, per-partner commodity access control. CFTC compliance logging to CloudWatch.
previous_plan.data_model: ClickHouse price_ticks with MergeTree partitioned by month. Redis HSET per contract_id with TTL 10s. No deduplication strategy specified for retransmissions.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision addressing two focus issues from the Round 1 audit: ISS-001 (CME mTLS certificate rotation undocumented) and ISS-002 (ClickHouse deduplication missing for CME retransmissions). Adding ACM Private CA certificate lifecycle documentation with dual-cert hot-swap renewal and ReplacingMergeTree deduplication key specification. Also addressing ISS-003 (Redis cache warm-up) by adding startup backfill goroutine. The financial data sensitivity and partner_facing exposure require compliance_context for CFTC and exchange licensing.

fix_report.action_taken: Documented CME client certificate rotation process using AWS ACM Private CA with 90-day cert lifecycle, automated renewal triggered 30 days before expiry, dual-cert hot-swap in feed adapter connection pool during renewal window, and zero-downtime rollover procedure in security_and_compliance and deployment_and_operations sections.
